Ayahs

كٓهيعٓصٓ (١)
Kaf, Ha, Ya, ‘Ayn, Suad. (1)
ذِكْرُ رَحْمَتِ رَبِّكَ عَبْدَهُۥ زَكَرِيَّآ (٢)
[This is] an account of your Lord’s mercy on His servant, Zechariah, (2)
إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُۥ نِدَآءً خَفِيًّا (٣)
when he called out to his Lord with a secret cry. (3)
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى وَهَنَ ٱلْعَظْمُ مِنِّى وَٱشْتَعَلَ ٱلرَّأْسُ شَيْبًا وَلَمْ أَكُنۢ بِدُعَآئِكَ رَبِّ شَقِيًّا (٤)
He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed my bones have become feeble, and my head has turned white with age, yet never have I, my Lord, been disappointed in supplicating You! (4)
وَإِنِّى خِفْتُ ٱلْمَوَٰلِىَ مِن وَرَآءِى وَكَانَتِ ٱمْرَأَتِى عَاقِرًا فَهَبْ لِى مِن لَّدُنكَ وَلِيًّا (٥)
Indeed I fear my kinsmen, after me, and my wife is barren. So grant me from Yourself an heir (5)
يَرِثُنِى وَيَرِثُ مِنْ ءَالِ يَعْقُوبَ ۖ وَٱجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا (٦)
who may inherit from me and inherit from the House of Jacob, and make him, my Lord, pleasing [to You]!’ (6)

Commentary

The Captivating Prayer of Zechariah Once again, we encounter the Disconnected Letters (Huruful-Muqatta'ah) at the beginning of this Surah (Kāf-Hā-Yā-‘Ayn-Sād).

Since we have previously discussed the interpretation of these Disconnected Letters in detail at the beginning of three different Surahs of the Quran (Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and Al-A'raf), we see no need for repetition here. [^1] What is necessary to add here is that regarding the Disconnected Letters of this Surah, two sets of narrations are found in Islamic sources:

First, narrations that consider each of these letters as an allusion to one of the

[^1]: Please refer to Volume 1 at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, Volume 2 at the beginning of Surah Al-Imran, and Volume 6 at the beginning of Surah Al-A'raf (Tafsir Nemouneh).

Great Names of Allah (al-Asma’ al-Husna): "Kāf" refers to Kāfī (The Sufficient), which is among the Great Names of Allah; "Hā" refers to Hādī (The Guide); "Yā" refers to Walī (The Guardian/Protector); "‘Ayn" refers to ‘Ālim (The All-Knowing); and "Sād" refers to Sādiq al-Wa‘d (The One Truthful in His Promise). [^1]

Second, narrations that interpret these Disconnected Letters in relation to the story of the uprising of Imam Hussain عليه السلام in Karbala: "Kāf" refers to "Karbala"; "Hā" refers to "Halāk" (the martyrdom/destruction) of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله household; "Yā" refers to "Yazid"; "‘Ayn" refers to the issue of "‘Atash" (thirst); and "Sād" refers to the "Sabr" (patience) and steadfastness of Hussain and his self-sacrificing companions. [^2]

Of course, as we have stated, the verses of the Quran are capable of carrying various meanings and sometimes express concepts from the past and future which, despite their diversity, do not contradict one another. However, if we restrict the meaning to a single interpretation, we might encounter difficulties regarding the circumstances and timing of the verse's revelation.

After mentioning the Disconnected Letters, the discourse begins with the story of Zechariah عليه السلام, stating: "This is a mention of the mercy of your Lord towards His servant Zechariah" (ذِكْرُ رَحْمَتِ رَبِّكَ عَبْدَهُ زَكَرِيَّا). [^3] At a time when he was deeply distressed and saddened by not having a child, he turned to the threshold of God: "When he called upon his Lord in a private call [where no one else could hear his voice]" (إِذْ نادى‌ رَبَّهُ نِداءً خَفِيًّا).

[^1] and [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 320. [^3]: In fact, the word "Dhikr" (mention) is the predicate (khabar) for an omitted subject (mubtada’), and the implied structure is: "Hādha dhikru rahmati rabbik" (This is a mention of the mercy of your Lord).

"He said: My Lord! Indeed, my bones—which are the pillars of my frame and the strongest parts of my body—have grown feeble" (قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي وَهَنَ الْعَظْمُ مِنِّي‌).

"And the flare of old age has engulfed all the hair on my head" (وَ اشْتَعَلَ الرَّأْسُ شَيْباً).

Likening the effects of old age to a flame that engulfs the entire head is a fascinating metaphor. Firstly, the characteristic of a flame is that it spreads quickly and consumes everything around it. Secondly, flames have a particular brilliance that attracts attention from afar. Thirdly, once fire has consumed a place, what remains is nothing but ashes.

Zechariah has likened the spread of old age and the whiteness of all his hair to the igniting of a fire and its brilliance, and the white ash it leaves behind. This is a very eloquent and beautiful metaphor.

He then adds: "My Lord, I have never been disappointed in my prayers to You" (وَ لَمْ أَكُنْ بِدُعائِكَ رَبِّ شَقِيًّا).

You have always accustomed me to the answering of my prayers in the past and have never deprived me. Now that I have become old and weak, I am even more deserving of You answering my prayer and not sending me back hopeless.

In fact, "shaqāwah" (wretchedness) here means toil and suffering; meaning, "I never fell into hardship or distress regarding my requests from You, for they were swiftly accepted by You."

He then explains his need as follows: "My Lord! Indeed, I fear my kinsmen

after me (lest they engage in corruption), and my wife is barren; so grant me from Yourself an heir" (وَ إِنِّي خِفْتُ الْمَوالِيَ مِنْ وَرائِي وَ كانَتِ امْرَأَتِي عاقِراً فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا).

"An heir who shall inherit from me and inherit from the family of Jacob; and make him, my Lord, pleasing [to You]" (يَرِثُنِي وَ يَرِثُ مِنْ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ وَ اجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا).

Key Points

1- What is meant by "inheritance" here?

Islamic commentators have discussed the answer to the above question extensively. One group believes "inheritance" here refers to the inheritance of wealth, while another group considers it an allusion to the station of Prophethood.

Some have also suggested the possibility that it refers to a comprehensive meaning encompassing both.

Many Shi'a scholars have chosen the first meaning, while a group of Sunni scholars chose the second, and some—like Sayyid Qutb in Fi Zilal and Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani—chose the third.

Those who restricted it to the inheritance of wealth relied on the literal appearance of the word "irth" (inheritance) in this sense; for when this word is used without other context, it means the inheritance of property. If we see it used for spiritual matters in some verses of the Quran—such as verse 32 of Surah Fatir: ثُمَّ أَوْرَثْنَا الْكِتابَ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَيْنا مِنْ عِبادِنا ("Then We gave the Book as inheritance to those We chose of Our servants")—it is due to the context existing in such cases.

Furthermore, it is understood from some narrations that at that time, the Children of Israel brought many gifts and offerings to the Ahbar (Jewish scholars), and Zechariah was the head of the scholars. [^1] Beyond this, Zechariah's wife, who was from the lineage of Solomon son of David, had inherited property given the extraordinary financial status of Solomon and David.

Zechariah feared that this wealth would fall into the hands of unworthy, opportunistic, and avaricious individuals, or the sinful and profligate, and thus become a source of corruption in society.

Therefore, he requested a righteous child from his Lord to oversee this wealth and spend it in the best way.

The famous narration from Fatimah al-Zahra عليه السلام, the pure daughter of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, who argued against the first Caliph to reclaim Fadak using the above verse, is another witness to this claim.

The late Tabarsi in the book Al-Ihtijaj narrates from the Lady of Islam عليه السلام as follows:

When the first Caliph decided to withhold Fadak from Fatimah عليه السلام and this news reached her, she came to him and said: "O Abu Bakr! Is it in the Book of Allah that you should inherit from your father and I should not inherit from my father? You have brought a monstrous thing! Have you intentionally abandoned the Book of Allah and cast it behind your backs? For it says regarding the story of John son of Zechariah: 'When he said: My Lord... grant me from Yourself an heir who shall inherit from me and inherit from the family of Jacob.'" [^1]

However, those who believe "inheritance" here is spiritual inheritance have clung to clues within the verse itself or external to it, such as:

1- It seems unlikely that a great Prophet like Zechariah, at that age, would have significant mental preoccupation regarding the heirs of his wealth, especially since after "inheriting from me," he adds the phrase: وَ اجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا ("and make him, my Lord, pleasing [to You]"). Undoubtedly, this phrase refers to the spiritual qualities of that heir.

2- In the upcoming verses, when God gives him the glad tidings of John's birth, He mentions great spiritual stations for him, including the station of Prophethood.

3- In Surah Al-Imran, verse 39, when explaining Zechariah's motivation for requesting a child, it indicates that he thought of this when he witnessed the stations of Mary, where by the grace of the Lord, heavenly food appeared before her prayer niche (هُنالِكَ دَعا زَكَرِيَّا رَبَّهُ قالَ رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً إِنَّكَ سَمِيعُ الدُّعاءِ).

4- In some hadiths from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, a matter is narrated that confirms inheritance here refers to the spiritual aspect. The summary of the hadith is as follows: Imam al-Sadiq narrates from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that Jesus son of Mary عليه السلام passed by a grave whose occupant was being punished. The following year he passed by again and noticed the occupant was no longer being punished. He asked his Lord about this, and divine revelation was sent to him that the occupant of this grave had a righteous son who repaired a road

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 323. [^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 324.

and gave shelter to an orphan; Allah forgave him because of his son's deed. Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "The legacy (mirath) of Allah the Almighty to His believing servant is to give him a child who, after him, is obedient to Allah's command." Then Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, while narrating this hadith, recited the verse related to Zechariah: فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا يَرِثُنِي وَ يَرِثُ مِنْ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ وَ اجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا. [^1]

If it is said that the literal appearance of the word inheritance is the inheritance of wealth, they will reply that this appearance is not definitive, as it is frequently used in the Quran for spiritual inheritance (such as verse 32 of Surah Fatir and verse 53 of Surah Ghafir).

Furthermore, even assuming it goes against the literal appearance, it poses no problem given the aforementioned clues.

However, proponents of the first view can answer these arguments by saying that the mental preoccupation of Zechariah, the great Prophet of God, was not the issue of wealth as a personal matter, but rather as a source of corruption or righteousness for society. For as mentioned above, many gifts and offerings were brought to the scholars and entrusted to Zechariah, and perhaps wealth also remained from his wife who was from the lineage of Solomon. It is obvious that the presence of an unworthy person at the head of these would cause great corruption, and this is what worried Zechariah.

As for the spiritual qualities mentioned for "John" in these verses and other verses of the Quran, they not only do not contradict this issue but are in harmony with it, for he wanted this great wealth to fall into the hands of a man of God to utilize it in the path of society's prosperity.

In our opinion, if we conclude from the sum of the above discussions that "inheritance" here has a broad meaning that includes both the inheritance of wealth and the inheritance of spiritual

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, pp. 323-324.

stations, it would not be incorrect, as there are clues for both sides. Given the preceding and following verses and the collection of narrations, this interpretation seems very plausible.

As for the phrase "Indeed, I fear my kinsmen after me" (إِنِّي خِفْتُ الْمَوالِيَ مِنْ وَرائِي), it is compatible with both meanings; for if corrupt individuals became the masters of that wealth, it was truly worrying, and likewise, if the spiritual leadership of the people fell into the hands of unworthy individuals, that too was a great cause for concern. Therefore, Zechariah's fear is justifiable in both cases.

The famous hadith of the Lady of Islam Fatimah al-Zahra عليه السلام is also compatible with this meaning.

[2- The meaning of the phrase: "When he called upon his Lord in a private call"]

Regarding the phrase "When he called upon his Lord in a private call" (إِذْ نادى‌ رَبَّهُ نِداءً خَفِيًّا), a question has been raised by commentators that "nādā" means to call out loudly, while "khafiyy" means quietly and secretly, and these two are not compatible. However, noting that "khafiyy" does not mean "quietly" but rather "privately/hiddenly," it is possible that Zechariah, in his place of seclusion where no one else was present, called upon God with a loud voice. Some have said this request was in the middle of the night when people were asleep. [^1]

Some have also taken the phrase "So he came out to his people from the prayer niche" (فَخَرَجَ عَلى‌ قَوْمِهِ مِنَ الْمِحْرابِ‌)—which appears in the upcoming verses—as evidence that this prayer occurred in a place of seclusion. [^2]

[3- The meaning of the phrase: "And inherit from the family of Jacob"]

The phrase "And inherit from the family of Jacob" (وَ يَرِثُ مِنْ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ‌) is because Zechariah's wife was the maternal aunt of Mary, the mother of Jesus, and that woman's lineage reached Jacob, for she was from the lineage of Solomon son of David, who was among the descendants of "Judah" son of "Jacob." [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 6, under the verse discussed. [^2]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 14, under the verse. [^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 6, under the verse.

Ayahs

يَـٰزَكَرِيَّآ إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلَـٰمٍ ٱسْمُهُۥ يَحْيَىٰ لَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا (٧)
‘O Zechariah! Indeed We give you the good news of a son, whose name is ‘‘John.’’ Never before have We made anyone his namesake.’ (7)
قَالَ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى غُلَـٰمٌ وَكَانَتِ ٱمْرَأَتِى عَاقِرًا وَقَدْ بَلَغْتُ مِنَ ٱلْكِبَرِ عِتِيًّا (٨)
He said, ‘My Lord! How shall I have a son, when my wife is barren, and I am already advanced in age?’ (8)
قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ رَبُّكَ هُوَ عَلَىَّ هَيِّنٌ وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْـًٔا (٩)
He said, ‘So shall it be. Your Lord has said, ‘‘It is simple for Me.’’ Certainly I created you before when you were nothing.’ (9)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱجْعَل لِّىٓ ءَايَةً ۚ قَالَ ءَايَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ ٱلنَّاسَ ثَلَـٰثَ لَيَالٍ سَوِيًّا (١٠)
He said, ‘My Lord! Appoint a sign for me.’ He said, ‘Your sign is that you will not speak to the people for three complete nights.’ (10)
فَخَرَجَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْمِحْرَابِ فَأَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِمْ أَن سَبِّحُوا۟ بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا (١١)
So he emerged before his people from the Temple, and signalled to them that they should glorify [Allah] morning and evening. (11)

Commentary

Zechariah Attained His Desire

These verses describe the granting of Zechariah’s prayer before the Lord—a response infused with His special grace and favor. It begins with the statement: "O Zechariah, indeed We bring you good tidings of a boy whose name is John (Yahya); We have not assigned to anyone before him [this] namesake" (يَا زَكَرِيَّا إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلَامٍ اسْمُهُ يَحْيَىٰ لَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُ مِن قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا).

How beautiful it is for Allah to accept His servant's prayer in such a manner, informing him of the fulfillment of his request through glad tidings, granting him a son in response to his plea for offspring, naming the son Himself, and adding that this child is unprecedented in certain respects.

For the phrase "We have not assigned to anyone before him [this] namesake," although it apparently means that no one had been given this name before, since a name alone is not proof of one's personality, it becomes clear that this name refers to the "named one" (the person himself). That is, someone possessing such distinctions as he had not existed before. Raghib has explicitly chosen this meaning in his book Al-Mufradat.

Undoubtedly, there were great prophets before Yahya, even greater than him, but there is no obstacle to Yahya having unique characteristics specific to himself, as will be mentioned later.

However, Zechariah, seeing that outward means were not favorable for achieving such a desire, sought clarification from the presence of the Lord and "said: My Lord! How can I have a son while my wife is barren and I have reached the extreme of old age?" (قَالَ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِي غُلَامٌ وَكَانَتِ امْرَأَتِي عَاقِرًا وَقَدْ بَلَغْتُ مِنَ الْكِبَرِ عِتِيًّا).

"Aaqir" (عاقر) is originally derived from the word "aqr" (عقر), meaning root and foundation, or meaning "restraint/confinement." The reason barren women are called "aaqir" is that their capacity for children has ended, or because the birth of a child is "confined" (restrained) within them.

"Itiyya" (عتي) refers to someone whose limbs have become dried up due to the passage of time—the state that occurs in humans at a very advanced age.

But soon, in response to his question, Zechariah received this message from the presence of Allah: "He said: Thus it is; your Lord says: It is easy for Me" (قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ رَبُّكَ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ) [1]. It is not a strange matter that a child should be born to an old man like you and a wife who is apparently barren.

[1]: The common view among commentators is that the phrase "kadhalika" (likewise/thus) implies "the matter is thus." There is also the possibility that "kadhalika" is linked to the following phrase, meaning: "Thus has your Lord spoken."

"I created you before, when you were nothing" (وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْئًا).

For the God who has the power to create everything from nothing, what room is there for wonder that He should grant you a child at this age and under these conditions?

Undoubtedly, the one giving the glad tidings and speaking in the first verse is Allah. However, regarding who the speaker is in the third verse under discussion (قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ رَبُّكَ), there are different views.

Some consider it the speech of the angels who were the means of bringing glad tidings to Zechariah, and verse 39 of Surah Al-Imran can be taken as evidence for this: "Then the angels called to him while he was standing in prayer in the sanctuary: 'Allah gives you glad tidings of Yahya...'" (فَنَادَتْهُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ وَهُوَ قَائِمٌ يُصَلِّي فِي الْمِحْرَابِ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكَ بِيَحْيَىٰ).

However, the apparent meaning is that the speaker of all these sentences is Allah, and there is no reason for us to deviate from the literal meaning. Even if the angels were the intermediaries for the glad tidings, there is no obstacle to Allah attributing the original message to Himself, especially since in verse 40 of the same Surah Al-Imran we read: "He said: Thus Allah does what He wills" (قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ اللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَاءُ).

In any case, upon hearing the above words, Zechariah became greatly encouraged and happy, and the light of hope filled his entire being. But since this message was very decisive and significant to him, he requested a sign from Allah for this event and "said: My Lord, appoint for me a sign" (قَالَ رَبِّ اجْعَل لِّي آيَةً).

Undoubtedly, Zechariah believed in the divine promise and his heart was at peace, but for greater reassurance—just as Abraham, who believed in the Resurrection, requested to witness the face of the Resurrection in this life so that his heart might find more tranquility—Zechariah requested such a sign and miracle from Allah.

"Allah said to him: Your sign is that you shall not speak to people for three nights [and days], being sound [in health]" (Only your tongue will move for the remembrance of Allah and supplication to Him) (قَالَ آيَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكُلِّمَ النَّاسَ ثَلَاثَ لَيَالٍ سَوِيًّا).

But what an amazing sign! A sign that, on one hand, was harmonious with his state of supplication and prayer, and on the other hand, cut him off from all creatures and linked him to Allah, so that in this state he might fulfill the gratitude for this great blessing and engage even more in the worship of God.

It is a clear sign when a person, despite having a healthy tongue and the power for any kind of supplication with the Lord, is unable to speak to people.

After these glad tidings and this clear sign, Zechariah came out from his place of worship to his people and signaled to them: "Glorify your Lord morning and evening" (فَخَرَجَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِ مِنَ الْمِحْرَابِ فَأَوْحَىٰ إِلَيْهِمْ أَن سَبِّحُوا بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا).

This was because the great blessing Allah had bestowed upon Zechariah encompassed all the people and would influence the future destiny of them all. For this reason, it was fitting that they all rise to glorify Allah and offer divine praise in gratitude for that blessing.

Furthermore, this gift, which was considered a miracle, could strengthen the foundations of faith in the hearts of individuals; this was another blessing in itself.

Key Points

Yahya, the Devout Prophet of Allah

The name "Yahya" appears a total of five times in the Surahs Al-Imran, Al-An'am, Maryam, and Al-Anbiya. He is one of the great prophets of Allah, and among his distinctions was that he reached the station of prophethood in childhood. Allah granted him such a clear intellect and radiant insight at that age that he became worthy of accepting this great office.

Among the characteristics this Prophet (peace be upon him) possessed, which the Quran mentions in Surah Al-Imran verse 39, is his description as "hasur" (حصور). As we mentioned in the commentary of that verse, "hasur" is derived from the root "hasr," meaning someone who is "encircled" or "restricted" in some way. Here, according to some narrations, it means one who abstains from marriage.

This was a distinction for him because it signifies the ultimate chastity and purity, or perhaps due to specific life conditions, he was forced to travel extensively to propagate the divine religion and, like Jesus Christ, was compelled to live celibately.

Another interpretation that seems plausible is that "hasur" in the above verse refers to someone who has abandoned worldly desires and passions, representing a high stage of asceticism [1]. In any case, it is understood from Islamic and Christian sources that Yahya was the maternal cousin of Jesus.

In Christian sources, it is explicitly stated that Yahya baptized Jesus (peace be upon him), and therefore he is called "John the Baptist" (Baptism is a special washing that Christians give to their children, believing it purifies them from sin). When Jesus declared his prophethood, Yahya believed in him.

Undoubtedly, Yahya did not have a specific unique heavenly book, and the fact that we read in the following verses, "O Yahya, take the Book with strength" (يَا يَحْيَىٰ خُذِ الْكِتَابَ بِقُوَّةٍ), refers to the "Torah," the book of Moses.

Of course, some people are followers of Yahya and attribute a book to him; perhaps the "Monotheistic Sabians" are the followers of Yahya [1]. Prophet Yahya and Prophet Jesus had commonalities: extraordinary asceticism, abstaining from marriage for the reasons mentioned, miraculous births, and a very close lineage.

It is understood from Islamic narrations that Imam Hussain (عليه السلام) and Yahya also had commonalities. Thus, it is narrated from Imam Ali ibn al-Hussain Zayn al-Abidin (عليه السلام) that he said:

"We went out with Hussain ibn Ali (عليه السلام); he did not descend at any station nor depart from it except that he mentioned Yahya ibn Zechariah and his killing. He would say: 'And how insignificant is the world to Allah, that the head of Yahya ibn Zechariah was sent as a gift to a harlot among the harlots of the Children of Israel'" [2].

The martyrdom of Imam Hussain (عليه السلام) was also similar in some respects to the martyrdom of Yahya (عليه السلام) (we will explain the manner of Yahya's killing later).

Also, the name of Hussain (عليه السلام), like the name of Yahya (عليه السلام), was unprecedented, and the duration of their gestation (while in their mother's womb) was shorter than usual.

[1]: A'lam al-Quran, p. 667. [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 3, p. 324.

Zechariah Attained His Desire

"Mihrab" (sanctuary/niche) is a specific place designated in a place of worship for the Imam or prominent individuals. Two reasons have been mentioned for its naming:

First, it is derived from the root "harb" (war), because the mihrab is in reality the place of battle against Satan and the whims of the self.

Second, "mihrab" linguistically means the highest point of a gathering, and since the location of the mihrab was at the top of the temple, it was called by this name.

Some say that the "mihrab" among the Children of Israel, contrary to what is common among us, was located at a point higher than the ground level, reached by several steps, and surrounded by walls so that those inside the mihrab were less visible from the outside. The phrase "Then he came out to his people from the sanctuary" (فَخَرَجَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِ مِنَ الْمِحْرَابِ) which we read in the above verses, considering the word "ala" (upon/over) which is usually used for a higher direction, supports this meaning.

Ayahs

يَـٰيَحْيَىٰ خُذِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِقُوَّةٍ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا (١٢)
‘O John!’ [We said,] ‘Hold on with power to the Book!’ And We gave him judgement while still a child, (12)
وَحَنَانًا مِّن لَّدُنَّا وَزَكَوٰةً ۖ وَكَانَ تَقِيًّا (١٣)
and a compassion and purity from Us. He was Godwary (13)
وَبَرًّۢا بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ وَلَمْ يَكُن جَبَّارًا عَصِيًّا (١٤)
and good to his parents, and was not self-willed or disobedient. (14)
وَسَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْهِ يَوْمَ وُلِدَ وَيَوْمَ يَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ يُبْعَثُ حَيًّا (١٥)
Peace be to him, the day he was born, and the day he dies, and the day he is raised alive! (15)

Commentary

The Outstanding Qualities of Yahya

In the preceding verses, we saw how Allah granted Yahya to Zakariya in his old age. Following that, in these verses, we first read the important divine command addressed to "Yahya": "O Yahya! Hold the Scripture with strength and power!" (يَا يَحْيَىٰ خُذِ الْكِتَابَ بِقُوَّةٍ).

It is well-known and famous among commentators that "the Scripture" (al-Kitab) here refers to the "Torah"; some have even claimed a consensus and agreement on this matter. [1]

[1]: Refer to Tafsir al-Alusi and Tafsir al-Qurtubi under the verse being discussed.

However, some have suggested the possibility that he had a book specific to himself (similar to the Psalms of David), though not a book containing a new law or a new religion. [1] But the first possibility seems stronger.

In any case, "holding the Scripture with strength and power" means implementing the Heavenly Book of the Torah and its contents with utmost decisiveness, firm resolve, and an iron will, acting upon all of it, and utilizing every material, spiritual, individual, and social force for its promotion and expansion.

Fundamentally, no "Book" or "School of Thought" can be implemented without the strength, power, and decisiveness of its followers. This is a lesson for all believers and all wayfarers on the path of "Allah."

After this command, He refers to the ten blessings that Allah had bestowed upon Yahya, or which he acquired through divine success:

1- "We gave him the command of prophethood, wisdom, intelligence, and insight in childhood" (وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا).

2- "We granted him mercy and affection toward the servants from Our presence" (وَحَنَانًا مِّن لَّدُنَّا). "Hanan" originally means mercy, compassion, affection, and the expression of love and inclination.

3- "We gave him purity of soul and spirit, and purity of action" (وَزَكَاةً).

Although commentators have offered various meanings for "Zakat"—some interpreting it as righteous deeds, some as obedience and sincerity, some as kindness to parents, some as a good reputation, and some as the purity of followers—it appears that "Zakat" has a broad meaning that encompasses all these forms of purity.

[1]: Refer to Tafsir al-Mizan under the verse being discussed.

4- "He was God-fearing" and avoided whatever was contrary to the command of his Lord (وَكَانَ تَقِيًّا).

5- "We made him kind, dutiful, and full of affection toward his parents" (وَبَرًّا بِوَالِدَيْهِ).

6- "He was not an oppressor, arrogant, or self-exalted before the creation of Allah" (وَلَمْ يَكُن جَبَّارًا).

7- "He was not disobedient or tainted by sin" (عَصِيًّا).

8, 9, and 10- And because he embodied these outstanding qualities and great honors, "Our peace be upon him on the day he was born, and Our peace be upon him on the day of his death, and peace be upon him on the day he shall be raised alive" (وَسَلَامٌ عَلَيْهِ يَوْمَ وُلِدَ وَيَوْمَ يَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ يُبْعَثُ حَيًّا).

Key Points

1- Hold the Heavenly Scripture with Strength and Power!

The word "strength" (quwwah) in the phrase يَا يَحْيَىٰ خُذِ الْكِتَابَ بِقُوَّةٍ, as we mentioned, has a very broad meaning, encompassing all material, spiritual, mental, and physical powers. This itself expresses the reality that preserving the divine religion, Islam, and the Quran is not possible with weakness, lethargy, negligence, or compromise; rather, it must be placed within the strong fortress of power, strength, and decisiveness.

Although the addressee here is "Yahya," this expression is seen in other instances in the Holy Quran regarding others as well:

In Surah Al-A'raf, verse 145, Moses is commissioned to take the Torah with strength: فَخُذْهَا بِقُوَّةٍ.

And in Surah Al-Baqarah, verses 63 and 93, the same address is seen regarding all the Children of Israel: خُذُوا مَا آتَيْنَاكُم بِقُوَّةٍ, which shows that this is a general decree for everyone, not just a specific person or persons.

Coincidentally, this same concept appears with a different expression in Surah Al-Anfal, verse 60: وَأَعِدُّوا لَهُم مَّا اسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن قُوَّةٍ: "And prepare against them whatever you are able of power [and strength] to strike terror into the enemies."

In any case, this verse is an answer to all those who imagine that something can be achieved from a position of weakness, or who wish to solve problems through compromise under all conditions.

2- Three Difficult Days in Human Destiny

The expression "Peace be upon him on the day he was born, and the day he dies, and the day he is raised alive" indicates that in the history of human life and his transition from one world to another, there are three difficult days: the day of stepping into this world (يَوْمَ وُلِدَ), the day of death and transition to the world of Barzakh (يَوْمَ يَمُوتُ), and the day of being raised in the other world (وَيَوْمَ يُبْعَثُ حَيًّا). Since these three transitional days are naturally faced with crises, Allah extends His peace and well-being to His special servants and takes them under His protection during these three stormy stages.

Although this expression appears in only two places in the Holy Quran—regarding Yahya (peace be upon him) and Jesus (peace be upon him)—the Quranic expression regarding Yahya has a special distinction, because the speaker of these words is Allah, whereas in the case of the Messiah (peace be upon him), the speaker is he himself.

It goes without saying that those who are in similar conditions to these two great figures will also be included in this peace.

Interestingly, in a narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام), we read:

"The most terrifying times for this creation are in three places: the day he is born and comes out of his mother's womb and sees the world; the day he dies and witnesses the Hereafter and its people; and the day he is raised alive and sees laws he did not see in the abode of this world. Allah granted peace to Yahya in these three places and secured his fear, saying: 'And peace be upon him...'" [1] O Lord, grant us peace in these three sensitive and critical stages.

3- Prophethood in Childhood

It is true that the period of the blossoming of human intellect usually has specific boundaries, but we know that there have always been exceptional individuals among humans. What is to prevent Allah from compressing this period for some of His servants for certain benefits and summarizing it in fewer years? Just as speaking usually requires a year or two to pass after birth, yet we know that Prophet Jesus (عليه السلام) began to speak in his very first days—and with very profound speech that, according to the normal course, was befitting of adults—as will come, God willing, in the commentary of the upcoming verses.

From here, it becomes clear that the objection some people raised against some of the Shiite Imams—as to why some of them reached the station of Imamate at a young age—is incorrect.

In a narration from one of the companions of Imam al-Jawad, Muhammad ibn Ali al-Naqi (عليه السلام), named Ali ibn Asbat, we read that he said: I came to him (while the Imam was young) and I stared intently at his stature so that I might memorize it and describe the details to my companions when I returned to Egypt. Right at that moment, while I was thinking this, he sat down (as if he had read my entire thought), turned to me, and said: "O Ali ibn Asbat! Allah has done in the matter of Imamate the same as He did in the matter of prophethood. Sometimes He says: وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا (We gave Yahya the command of prophethood, wisdom, and insight in childhood), and sometimes He says regarding humans: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَبَلَغَ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً (Until, when he reaches full maturity and reaches forty years...). Therefore, just as it is possible for Allah to give wisdom to a person in childhood, it is within His power to give it at forty years." [1] Furthermore, this verse is a crushing response to critics who say that Ali (عليه السلام) was not the first among men to believe in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) because he was a ten-year-old child on that day, and the faith of a ten-year-old child is not accepted.

It is also not out of place to mention here that in a narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام), we read that a group of children came to him during his childhood and said: "Come, let us go and play!" He replied: "We were not created for play." It was then that Allah said regarding him: وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا. [1] Of course, it must be noted that "play" (la'ib) here refers to vain pastimes or, in other words, futility; however, sometimes play and games pursue a goal—a logical and rational goal—and certainly such games are excluded from this judgment.

4- The Martyrdom of Yahya

Not only was the birth of Yahya amazing, but his death was also strange in some respects. Most Muslim historians, as well as famous Christian sources, have narrated the account of this martyrdom as follows (though slight differences in details are seen among them):

Yahya became a victim of the illicit relations of one of the tyrants of his time with one of his close relatives (mahram). Herod, the hedonistic king of Palestine, fell in love with Herodias, his brother's daughter, and her beauty held his heart captive in a fiery love; thus, he decided to marry her!

This news reached the great Prophet of Allah, Yahya. He explicitly declared that this marriage was illegitimate and contrary to the commands of the Torah, and that he would rise to oppose such an act.

The clamor of this issue spread throughout the city and reached the ears of that girl, Herodias. Seeing Yahya as the greatest obstacle in her path, she decided to take revenge on him at a suitable opportunity and remove this obstacle from the path of her desires.

She increased her connection with her uncle and used her beauty as a trap for him, influencing him to such an extent that one day Herod said to her: "Ask of me any wish you have, and it shall surely be fulfilled."

Herodias said: "I want nothing but the head of Yahya! For he has made our names the talk of the town, and all the people have sat in judgment of us. If you want my heart to be at peace and my mind to be happy, you must do this!" Herod, who was madly in love with that woman, surrendered without regard for the consequences of this act, and it was not long before the head of Yahya was brought before that wicked woman. But the painful consequences of this act eventually overtook him. [1] In Islamic hadiths, we read that the Master of Martyrs, Imam Husayn (عليه السلام), used to say:

"Among the trivialities of the world is that the head of Yahya ibn Zakariya was taken as a gift to a wicked woman from among the women of the Children of Israel."

Meaning, my circumstances and those of Yahya are similar in this regard as well, for one of the goals of my uprising is to fight against the shameful acts of the tyrant of my time, Yazid.

[1]: From some of the Gospels and certain narrations, it is understood that Herod married his brother's wife, which was forbidden in the law of the Torah, and Yahya severely rebuked him for this. Then that woman, through the beauty of her daughter, compelled Herod to kill Yahya (Gospel of Matthew, Chapter 14; Gospel of Mark, Chapter 6, verse 17 onwards).

Ayahs

وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَرْيَمَ إِذِ ٱنتَبَذَتْ مِنْ أَهْلِهَا مَكَانًا شَرْقِيًّا (١٦)
And mention in the Book Mary, when she withdrew from her family to an easterly place. (16)
فَٱتَّخَذَتْ مِن دُونِهِمْ حِجَابًا فَأَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهَا رُوحَنَا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَهَا بَشَرًا سَوِيًّا (١٧)
Thus did she seclude herself from them, whereupon We sent to her Our Spirit and he became incarnate for her as a well-proportioned human. (17)
قَالَتْ إِنِّىٓ أَعُوذُ بِٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مِنكَ إِن كُنتَ تَقِيًّا (١٨)
She said, ‘I seek the protection of the All-beneficent from you, should you be Godwary!’ (18)
قَالَ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ رَسُولُ رَبِّكِ لِأَهَبَ لَكِ غُلَـٰمًا زَكِيًّا (١٩)
He said, ‘I am only a messenger of your Lord that I may give you a pure son.’ (19)
قَالَتْ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى غُلَـٰمٌ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِى بَشَرٌ وَلَمْ أَكُ بَغِيًّا (٢٠)
She said, ‘How shall I have a child seeing that no human being has ever touched me, nor have I been unchaste?’ (20)
قَالَ كَذَٰلِكِ قَالَ رَبُّكِ هُوَ عَلَىَّ هَيِّنٌ ۖ وَلِنَجْعَلَهُۥٓ ءَايَةً لِّلنَّاسِ وَرَحْمَةً مِّنَّا ۚ وَكَانَ أَمْرًا مَّقْضِيًّا (٢١)
He said, ‘So shall it be. Your Lord says, ‘‘It is simple for Me.’’ And so that We may make him a sign for mankind and a mercy from Us, and it is a matter [already] decided.’ (21)

Commentary

The Beginning of the Birth of Messiah (عليه السلام):

After recounting the story of Yahya (عليه السلام), the discourse shifts to the story of the birth of Jesus (عليه السلام) and the account of his mother Maryam, as there is a very close connection between these two events.

If the birth of Yahya from an old, frail father and a barren mother was wondrous, the birth of Jesus from a mother without a father is even more wondrous.

If reaching the station of intellect and prophethood in childhood is amazing, speaking in the cradle—and that too regarding the Book and prophethood—is even more amazing.

In any case, both are signs of the power of the Great Allah, one greater than the other. Coincidentally, both pertain to individuals who were very closely related by lineage, as the mother of Yahya was the sister of Maryam’s mother; both were barren women who lived in longing for a righteous child.

The first verse under discussion says: "And mention Maryam in the Book (the Quran), when she withdrew from her family to an eastern place" (وَ اذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ مَرْيَمَ إِذِ انْتَبَذَتْ مِنْ أَهْلِها مَكاناً شَرْقِيًّا).

In truth, she wanted to find a secluded place, free from any distractions, to engage in intimate prayer with her Lord, ensuring nothing would divert her from the remembrance of the Beloved. For this reason, she chose the eastern side of Jerusalem (that great temple), which was perhaps a quieter location or cleaner and more suitable in terms of sunlight.

The word intabadhat (انتبذت) is derived from the root nabdh (نبذ), which according to Raghib means "to cast away insignificant objects." This expression in the verse may imply that Maryam withdrew from the crowd in a humble and anonymous manner, free from any action that would draw attention, and chose that location in the House of God for worship.

At this moment, Maryam "screened herself off from them" so that her place of seclusion would be complete in every respect (فَاتَّخَذَتْ مِنْ دُونِهِمْ حِجاباً). In this sentence, it is not explicitly stated for what purpose this screen (hijab) was intended—whether it was so she could worship her Lord and engage in intimate prayer more freely and without distraction, or whether she intended to wash and perform a ritual bath (ghusl). The verse remains silent on this matter.

In any case, "Then We sent to her Our Spirit (one of the great angels), and he appeared to her in the form of a perfect, flawless, and handsome human being" (فَأَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْها رُوحَنا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَها بَشَراً سَوِيًّا).

It is evident what state Maryam would experience at this moment—a Maryam who had always lived chastely, was raised in the lap of the pure, and was a proverb of modesty and piety among the people. What fear and terror must have seized her upon seeing such a sight: a beautiful male stranger finding his way into her private sanctuary? Therefore, she immediately "cried out: 'I seek refuge in the Most Merciful (Allah) from you, if you are God-fearing'" (قالَتْ إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِالرَّحْمنِ مِنْكَ إِنْ كُنْتَ تَقِيًّا).

This was the first tremor that surged through Maryam’s entire being.

Invoking the name of the Most Merciful (al-Rahman) and describing Him by His general mercy on one hand, and encouraging the stranger to piety and God-fearingness on the other, were all intended to restrain that unknown person if he had any ill intentions. Above all, seeking refuge in Allah—the One who is the support of man in the most difficult circumstances and against whose power no power can stand—would resolve the problem.

After saying this, Maryam awaited the reaction of the unknown man, an anticipation mixed with great terror and anxiety. However, this state did not last long; the stranger began to speak and explained his great mission and message, "He said: 'I am only a messenger from your Lord!'" (قالَ إِنَّما أَنَا رَسُولُ رَبِّكِ). This sentence was like water poured on a fire, bringing tranquility to Maryam’s pure heart.

But this tranquility did not last long either, for he immediately added: "I have come to bestow upon you a pure son—pure in character, body, and soul!" (لِأَهَبَ لَكِ غُلاماً زَكِيًّا).

Upon hearing these words, a violent tremor seized Maryam’s being, and once again she fell into deep anxiety and "said: 'How can I have a son when no man has touched me, nor have I ever been unchaste?'" (قالَتْ أَنَّى يَكُونُ لِي غُلامٌ وَ لَمْ يَمْسَسْنِي بَشَرٌ وَ لَمْ أَكُ بَغِيًّا).

At that moment, she was thinking only of natural causes, believing that for a woman to have a child, there are only two ways: either marriage and choosing a spouse, or impurity and deviation. "I, who know myself better than anyone, have neither chosen a spouse nor have I ever been a deviant woman; it has never been heard of for someone to have a child without these two!"

But soon the storm of this renewed anxiety subsided upon hearing another statement from the Lord’s messenger. He explicitly "said: 'So it shall be; your Lord says: It is easy for Me'" (قالَ كَذلِكِ قالَ رَبُّكِ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ). "You, who are well aware of My power; you, who have seen heavenly fruits by your prayer niche in a season when nothing like them existed in the world; you, who have heard the voices of angels testifying to your purity; you, who know your forefather Adam was created from dust—why are you surprised by this news?" Then he added: "And We wish to make him a sign and a miracle for the people" (وَ لِنَجْعَلَهُ آيَةً لِلنَّاسِ).

"And We wish to make him a mercy from Us for the servants" (وَ رَحْمَةً مِنَّا).

In any case, "this is a matter already decreed" and there is no room for further discussion (وَ كانَ أَمْراً مَقْضِيًّا).

Key Points

1- What is meant by "The Spirit of Allah"?

Almost all famous commentators have interpreted "the Spirit" (al-Ruh) here as Gabriel (Jibril), the great angel of Allah. He is referred to as "Spirit" because he is spiritual and a life-giving being, as he carries the Divine message to the prophets, which revives all worthy human beings. The attribution of "Spirit" to Allah here is a proof of the greatness and nobility of this spirit, as one type of attribution is the "honorific attribution" (idafah tashrifiyyah).

Furthermore, it is inferred from this verse that the descent of Gabriel was not exclusive to prophets. While he descended exclusively upon them for the purpose of revelation and bringing Sharia and Divine Books, there is no obstacle to him meeting with non-prophets to deliver other messages (such as the aforementioned message to Maryam).

2- What is "Tamathul" (Appearing in form)?

Tamathul (تمثل) is originally derived from the root muthul (مثول), meaning to stand before a person or thing. A mumathal is something that appears in the form of another. Therefore, "he appeared to her as a well-proportioned man" (فَتَمَثَّلَ لَها بَشَراً سَوِيًّا) means that the Divine angel took the form of a human.

Undoubtedly, the meaning of this is not that Gabriel transformed into a human in essence and nature, for such a transformation is impossible. Rather, it means he appeared in the form of a human, even though his inner nature remained that of an angel. However, Maryam, being unaware at first, perceived that a human being was before her in both form and nature.

In Islamic narrations and histories, tamathul in the broad sense of the word is frequently seen. Among these instances: On the day when the polytheists gathered in Dar al-Nadwa and were plotting to destroy the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), Iblis appeared in the clothing of a well-wishing and benevolent old man and began to incite the leaders of the Quraysh.

Or, the world and its inner reality appeared as a beautiful, alluring woman before Ali (عليه السلام), but found no power to influence him—a story that is detailed and well-known.

We also read in narrations that a person's wealth, children, and deeds manifest before them at the time of death in various specific forms.

Or, human deeds are personified in the grave and on the Day of Resurrection, each appearing in a specific shape. In all these cases, the concept of tamathul is that a thing or person takes the outward shape of another, not that its essence or inner nature has changed [1].

[1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 14, p. 37.

Ayahs

۞ فَحَمَلَتْهُ فَٱنتَبَذَتْ بِهِۦ مَكَانًا قَصِيًّا (٢٢)
Thus she conceived him, then withdrew with him to a distant place. (22)
فَأَجَآءَهَا ٱلْمَخَاضُ إِلَىٰ جِذْعِ ٱلنَّخْلَةِ قَالَتْ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى مِتُّ قَبْلَ هَـٰذَا وَكُنتُ نَسْيًا مَّنسِيًّا (٢٣)
The birth pangs brought her to the trunk of a date palm. She said, ‘I wish I had died before this and become a forgotten thing, beyond recall.’ (23)
فَنَادَىٰهَا مِن تَحْتِهَآ أَلَّا تَحْزَنِى قَدْ جَعَلَ رَبُّكِ تَحْتَكِ سَرِيًّا (٢٤)
Thereupon he called her from below her [saying,] ‘Do not grieve! Your Lord has made a spring to flow at your feet. (24)
وَهُزِّىٓ إِلَيْكِ بِجِذْعِ ٱلنَّخْلَةِ تُسَـٰقِطْ عَلَيْكِ رُطَبًا جَنِيًّا (٢٥)
Shake the trunk of the palm tree, freshly picked dates will drop upon you. (25)
فَكُلِى وَٱشْرَبِى وَقَرِّى عَيْنًا ۖ فَإِمَّا تَرَيِنَّ مِنَ ٱلْبَشَرِ أَحَدًا فَقُولِىٓ إِنِّى نَذَرْتُ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ صَوْمًا فَلَنْ أُكَلِّمَ ٱلْيَوْمَ إِنسِيًّا (٢٦)
Eat, drink, and be comforted. Then if you see any human, say, ‘‘Indeed I have vowed a fast to the All-beneficent, so I will not speak to any human today.’’ ’ (26)

Commentary

Mary Amidst the Fiercest Storms of Life

"Finally, Mary became pregnant," and that promised child took place in her womb (فَحَمَلَتْهُ).

As to how this child came into existence—whether Gabriel breathed into her garment or her mouth—the Quran does not speak of it, as there was no need for it, although the words of the commentators vary in this regard.

In any case, "this matter caused her to withdraw from the Holy House (Bayt al-Maqdis) to a remote place" (فَانْتَبَذَتْ بِهِ مَكاناً قَصِيًّا).

In this state, she lived between fear and hope, a state of anxiety coupled with joy. At times, she thought that this pregnancy would eventually be revealed; even if she stayed away for a few days or months from those who knew her and lived incognito in this spot, what would happen in the end?! Who would accept from her that a woman could become pregnant without having a husband unless she were unchaste? What should she do with this accusation? Indeed, for a girl who for years had been the symbol of purity, chastity, piety, and virtue, and a model in worship and servitude to Allah—one whom the ascetics and worshippers of the Children of Israel took pride in guardianship of since her childhood, who was raised under the supervision of a great Prophet, and whose moral excellence and reputation for sanctity had spread everywhere—it was extremely painful to feel one day that all her spiritual capital was at risk, and that she was caught in a whirlpool of an accusation considered among the worst of accusations. This was the third tremor that shook her being.

But on the other hand, she felt that this child was the promised Prophet of Allah and a great heavenly gift. How could the God who gave her the glad tidings of such a child and created him in such a miraculous manner leave her alone? Is it possible He would not defend her against such an accusation? She who had always experienced His grace and seen His hand of mercy over her head.

Regarding how long Mary's pregnancy lasted, there is discussion among the commentators, although it is mentioned cryptically in the Quran. Some have considered it one hour, some nine hours, some six months, some seven months, some eight months, and some nine months like other women; however, this subject does not significantly affect the purpose of this story.

The narrations in this field are also varied.

As for where this "remote" (قصى) place was, many believe it was the city of "Nazareth," and perhaps in that city, she constantly stayed at home and rarely stepped outside.

Whatever it was, the period of pregnancy ended, and the stormy moments of Mary's life began. The severe pain of childbirth seized her, such that it drove her from the inhabited area to the wilderness—a wilderness empty of people, dry, waterless, and without shelter! Although in this state women seek refuge with their acquaintances and friends so they may help them with the birth of the child, because Mary's situation was exceptional and she never wanted anyone to witness her delivery, she took the path to the wilderness as the labor pains began.

The Quran says in this regard: "The pains of childbirth drove her to the trunk of a palm tree" (فَأَجاءَهَا الْمَخاضُ إِلى‌ جِذْعِ النَّخْلَةِ).

The expression "jidh‘ al-nakhlah" (جِذْعِ النَّخْلَةِ), considering that "jidh‘" means the trunk of a tree, indicates that only a torso of that tree remained; that is, it was a dried-up tree [1]. In this state, a storm of grief and sorrow overwhelmed Mary's entire pure being. She felt that the moment she feared had arrived—the moment when everything hidden would be revealed, and the barrage of arrows of accusation from faithless people would be directed at her.

This storm was so severe and this burden weighed so heavily on her shoulders that she involuntarily "said: Oh, would that I had died before this and had been a thing forgotten, out of sight!" (قالَتْ يا لَيْتَنِي مِتُّ قَبْلَ هذا وَ كُنْتُ نَسْياً مَنْسِيًّا).

Naturally, it was not only the fear of future accusations that squeezed Mary's heart, although this was her primary preoccupation; rather, other difficulties and calamities, such as giving birth without a midwife or a friend and helper in a wilderness all alone, the lack of a place to rest, water to drink, food to eat, and means to care for the newborn—these were matters that shook her severely.

And those who say, "How could Mary, with her faith and monotheistic insight, having seen all that divine grace and kindness, utter such a sentence as 'Would that I had died and been forgotten'?" have never formed a picture of Mary's state at that hour in their minds. If they themselves were afflicted with a small fraction of these problems, they would become so distraught that they would forget even themselves.

But this state did not last long, and that same bright point of hope that always existed in the depths of her heart began to shine. "Suddenly a voice reached her ears rising from below her, saying clearly: Do not grieve; look closely, your Lord has caused a stream of sweet water to flow from beneath you" (فَناداها مِنْ تحتها أَلَّا تَحْزَنِي قَدْ جَعَلَ رَبُّكِ تَحْتَكِ سَرِيًّا).

"And cast a glance above your head; see how the dried trunk has turned into a fruitful palm tree whose fruits have adorned its branches. Shake the trunk of this palm tree toward yourself; it will drop fresh ripe dates upon you" (وَ هُزِّي إِلَيْكِ بِجِذْعِ النَّخْلَةِ تُساقِطْ عَلَيْكِ رُطَباً جَنِيًّا).

"Eat of this delicious and strengthening food, and drink of that sweet water" (فَكُلِي وَ اشْرَبِي‌). "And let your eyes be comforted by this newborn" (وَ قَرِّي عَيْناً).

"And if you are concerned about the future, be at ease; whenever you see any human and they ask you for an explanation in this regard, say by gesture: I have vowed a fast to the Compassionate (Allah)—a fast of silence—and for this reason, I shall not speak to any human today" (فَإِمَّا تَرَيِنَّ مِنَ الْبَشَرِ أَحَداً فَقُولِي إِنِّي نَذَرْتُ لِلرَّحْمنِ صَوْماً فَلَنْ أُكَلِّمَ الْيَوْمَ إِنْسِيًّا).

In short, there is no need for you to defend yourself; He who gave you this newborn has also taken the duty of defense upon Himself.

Therefore, be at ease in every respect, and let not grief and sorrow enter your mind.

These successive events, which shone like bright sparks in an exceptionally dark and gloomy atmosphere, illuminated her entire heart, and a state of pleasant tranquility came over her.

Key Points

1- Mary Was Tempered Through Difficulties

The events that Mary went through in this short period and the wondrous scenes of Allah's grace that occurred for her certainly prepared her for raising an Arch-Prophet (Ulul-'Azm), so that she could well fulfill her maternal duty in this momentous task.

The course of events took her to the final stage of difficulties, such that she saw no more than one step between herself and the end of life; but suddenly the page turned, everything rushed to her aid, and she stepped into an environment that was calm and secure in every respect.

The phrase "Shake the trunk of the palm tree toward yourself" (هُزِّي إِلَيْكِ بِجِذْعِ النَّخْلَةِ), which commands Mary to shake the palm tree to benefit from its fruit, gave this instructive lesson to her and to all humans: that even in the most difficult moments of life, one must not give up effort and striving.

This statement is an answer to those who think: what need was there for Mary, who had just given birth, to rise and shake the palm tree? Would it not have been better for the God by whose command the spring of sweet water gushed near Mary and by whose command the dried tree became fruitful, to send a breeze to shake the branch of the tree and drop the dates around Mary? What happened that when Mary was healthy, heavenly fruit was present by her prayer niche (Mihrab), but now that she is caught in this severe storm, she must pick the fruit herself?! Yes, this divine command to Mary shows that unless there is a movement from us, there will be no blessing (Barakah). In other words, everyone must exert their maximum effort when problems arise and ask Allah for what is beyond their power. As the poet says: "Rise and shake the palm tree / So you may be satiated and reach its fruit! For that Mary, until she shook the tree / No dates fell by her side!"

2- Why Mary Requested Death from Allah

Undoubtedly, requesting death from Allah is not a correct act, but sometimes in human life, severe events occur where the taste of life becomes completely bitter and unpleasant, especially where a person sees their sacred goals or their honor and dignity at risk and has no power to defend against it. In such cases, one sometimes requests death to escape spiritual torture.

Mary, too, because in the first moments the thought occurred to her that all her reputation and dignity before the foolish people would be endangered by the birth of this child, it was here that she wished for death and to be forgotten. This itself is proof that she loved chastity and purity even more than her life and valued her reputation more than her existence.

However, such thoughts, which perhaps occurred in very brief moments, did not last long. Upon seeing the two divine miracles—the gushing of the spring and the fruiting of the dried palm tree—all these thoughts left her soul, and the light of assurance and tranquility filled her entire heart.

3- Answer to a Question

Some ask: if miracles are specific to Prophets and Imams, then how did such miracles appear for Mary?

Some commentators, to solve this problem, have taken them as part of the miracles of Jesus that occurred as a prelude, and they refer to it as "Irhas" (Irhas means a preliminary miracle).

But there is no need for such answers, because the appearance of supernatural events (Khariq al-'Adat) for those other than Prophets and Imams has no obstacle. This is what we call "Karamah" (Grace/Wonder).

A miracle (Mu'jizah) is that which is accompanied by "Tahaddi" (a challenge to match it) and accompanied by a claim of Prophethood or Imamate.

4- The Fast of Silence

The literal meaning of the above verses shows that Mary was commanded to be silent for a certain benefit and, by Allah's command, refrained from speaking during this specific period so that her infant, Jesus, would speak and defend her purity, which was more effective and captivating in every way.

However, it appears from the expression of the verse that the vow of silence was a recognized practice for those people and that community; for this reason, they did not criticize her for this act.

But this type of fasting is not prescribed in the Sharia of Islam.

It is narrated from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام in a hadith: "The fast of silence is forbidden (Haram)" [1]. This is due to the difference in conditions at that time compared to the time of the advent of Islam.

But of course, one of the etiquettes of a complete fast in Islam is that a person, while fasting, protects their tongue from being tainted by sin and disliked acts (Makruhat), and likewise keeps their eyes from any impurity. As we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Fasting is not from food and drink alone; Mary said: 'I have vowed a fast to the Compassionate,' meaning silence. Therefore, (when you are fasting) guard your tongues, lower your gazes, do not envy one another, and do not dispute" [2].

5- A Strengthening Food

From the fact that it is explicitly stated in the above verses that Allah made Mary's food at the time of the infant's birth "rutab" (fresh dates), commentators have inferred that one of the best foods for women after childbirth is fresh dates.

In Islamic hadiths, this point is also explicitly mentioned: We read from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, narrating from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: "The first thing a woman in postnatal state (Nufasa) should eat is fresh dates, for Allah the Almighty said to Mary عليه السلام: 'And shake the trunk of the palm tree toward yourself; it will drop fresh ripe dates upon you'" [1]. From the end of this same hadith, it is understood that eating this food is not only effective for the mother but will also affect her milk.

It is even understood from some narrations that the best food for a pregnant woman and her medicine is fresh dates (ما تاكل الحامل من شى‌ء و لا تتداوى به افضل من الرطب) [2]. But certainly, moderation in everything, even in this matter, must be observed, as is understood from some narrations regarding this.

It is also understood that if fresh dates are not found, regular dates can be used.

Nutritionists say: the abundant sugar found in dates is among the healthiest sugars, which even in many cases, those suffering from diabetes can use.

These same scientists say: they have discovered 13 vital substances and five types of vitamins in dates, the combination of which has turned the date into a rich food source [3]. And we know that women in such a state have a severe need for strengthening and vitamin-rich food.

With the advancement of medical science, the medicinal importance of dates has also been proven. In dates, there is "calcium," which is the main factor for strengthening bones; there is also "phosphorus," which is one of the main elements forming the brain and prevents nerve weakness and fatigue; and "potassium" is also present, the lack of which in the body is considered the true cause of stomach ulcers [1].

[1] (Footnote from text): Jidh‘ (on the weight of dhibh) is originally from the root "jadh‘" (on the weight of man‘) meaning to cut or sever. [1] (Footnote from end): Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 7, p. 390. [2] (Footnote from end): Man La Yahduruhu al-Faqih, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 332. [1] & [2] (Footnote from end): Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 330. [3] (Footnote from end): From the book "The First University and the Last Prophet," Vol. 7, p. 65.
