Ayahs

فَأَتَتْ بِهِۦ قَوْمَهَا تَحْمِلُهُۥ ۖ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ لَقَدْ جِئْتِ شَيْـًٔا فَرِيًّا (٢٧)
Then carrying him she brought him to her people. They said, ‘O Mary, you have certainly come up with an odd thing! (27)
يَـٰٓأُخْتَ هَـٰرُونَ مَا كَانَ أَبُوكِ ٱمْرَأَ سَوْءٍ وَمَا كَانَتْ أُمُّكِ بَغِيًّا (٢٨)
O sister of Aaron[’s lineage]! Your father was not an evil man, nor was your mother unchaste.’ (28)
فَأَشَارَتْ إِلَيْهِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ مَن كَانَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّا (٢٩)
Thereat she pointed to him. They said, ‘How can we speak to one who is yet a baby in the cradle?’ (29)
قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّا (٣٠)
He said, ‘Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet. (30)
وَجَعَلَنِى مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ وَأَوْصَـٰنِى بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّا (٣١)
He has made me blessed, wherever I may be, and He has enjoined me to [maintain] the prayer and to [pay] the zakat as long as I live, (31)
وَبَرًّۢا بِوَٰلِدَتِى وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِى جَبَّارًا شَقِيًّا (٣٢)
and to be good to my mother, and He has not made me self-willed and wretched. (32)
وَٱلسَّلَـٰمُ عَلَىَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدتُّ وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّا (٣٣)
Peace to me the day I was born, and the day I die, and the day I am raised alive.’ (33)

Commentary

Messiah Speaks in the Cradle

"At last, Maryam returned from the wilderness to the settlement while carrying her child in her arms, and she came to her kinsmen and relatives" (فَأَتَتْ بِهِ قَوْمَها تَحْمِلُهُ‌).

When they saw a newborn infant in her arms, their mouths opened in astonishment. Those who knew Maryam’s history of chastity and had heard of her reputation for piety and nobility became deeply concerned, to the point that some fell into doubt, while others, who were hasty in judgment and arbitration, began to reproach and rebuke her. They said: "What a pity for that brilliant record to be stained with such impurity! And a hundred pities for that pure lineage to be so defamed." They said: "O Maryam! You have certainly done a very strange and evil thing!" (قالُوا يا مَرْيَمُ لَقَدْ جِئْتِ شَيْئاً فَرِيًّا) [^1]. Some turned to her and said: "O sister of Harun! Your father was not an evil man, nor was your mother ever unchaste" (يا أُخْتَ هارُونَ ما كانَ أَبُوكِ امْرَأَ سَوْءٍ وَ ما كانَتْ أُمُّكِ بَغِيًّا).

With such pure parents, what is this state we see you in? What evil did you see in your father’s way or your mother’s method that you turned away from it?! The fact that they addressed Maryam as "O sister of Harun" has led to various interpretations among commentators. However, what seems most correct is that Harun was a pure and righteous man, so much so that he had become a proverb among the Children of Israel; whenever they wanted to introduce someone as pure, they would say: "He/she is the brother/sister of Harun." The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan narrated this meaning in a short hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله [^1]. In another hadith found in the book Sa'd al-Su'ud, we read:

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent Mughirah to Najran (to invite the Christians to Islam). A group of Christians said (by way of criticizing the Quran): "Do you not read in your Book 'O sister of Harun,' while we know that if Harun the brother of Musa is intended, there was a vast distance [of time] between Maryam and Harun?"

Since Mughirah could not provide an answer, he asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله about the matter. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Why did you not tell them in response that it was customary among the Children of Israel to attribute virtuous people to the Prophets and the righteous?" [^2]

At this moment, Maryam remained silent by Allah's command. The only thing she did was point to her infant, 'Isa (فَأَشارَتْ إِلَيْهِ‌). But this action increased their astonishment even more, and perhaps a group took it as mockery and became angry, saying: "Maryam, with such an act you have done, you even mock your own people?"

In any case, "they said to her: 'How can we speak to a child who is in the cradle?'" (قالُوا كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ مَنْ كانَ فِي الْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّا).

Commentators have discussed extensively the word "كان" (was), which denotes the past tense. However, it appears that this word here refers to the fixity and necessity of an existing attribute. To put it more clearly, they said to Maryam: "How can we speak with a child who has been and is in the cradle?"

Evidence for this meaning is found in other verses of the Quran, such as: كُنْتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ‌: "You were [and are] the best nation brought forth for the benefit of human society" (Surah Al-Imran, 110). Certainly, the phrase "كنتم" (you were) here does not mean the past tense, but rather expresses the continuity and fixity of these attributes for the Islamic community.

They also discussed "مهد" (cradle), noting that 'Isa had not yet reached a physical cradle; rather, the appearance of the verses suggests that as soon as Maryam entered among the crowd while 'Isa was in her arms, this exchange took place between her and the people.

However, considering the meaning of the word "مهد" in the Arabic language, the answer to this question becomes clear. The word mahd—as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat—means a place prepared for a child, whether it be a cradle, the mother's lap, or a bed. Both mahd and mihad in the language mean "the prepared and spread-out place" (for rest and sleep).

In any case, the crowd became anxious and perhaps even enraged upon hearing Maryam's words, such that according to some narrations, they said to one another: "Her mockery and derision are harder and heavier for us than her deviation from the path of chastity!"

But this state did not last long, for that newborn child opened his mouth to speak and "said: 'Indeed, I am the servant of Allah'" (قالَ إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللَّهِ‌).

"He has granted me the Heavenly Book" (آتانِيَ الْكِتابَ‌).

"And He has made me a Prophet" (وَ جَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا).

"And Allah has made me a blessed being (a being useful in every respect for the servants wherever I may be)" (وَ جَعَلَنِي مُبارَكاً أَيْنَ ما كُنْتُ‌). "And He has enjoined upon me prayer and zakat as long as I live" (وَ أَوْصانِي بِالصَّلاةِ وَ الزَّكاةِ ما دُمْتُ حَيًّا).

And also "He has made me dutiful, appreciative, and benevolent toward my mother" (وَ بَرًّا بِوالِدَتِي‌) [^1] "and He has not made me an arrogant tyrant or wretched" (وَ لَمْ يَجْعَلْنِي جَبَّاراً شَقِيًّا). "Jabbar" (Tyrant/Arrogant) refers to one who claims all kinds of rights over people for himself but recognizes no right for anyone over himself! Also, "Jabbar" refers to one who strikes and destroys people out of anger and wrath and does not follow the command of reason, or one who wishes to compensate for his own deficiency and lack by claiming greatness and pride—all of which are prominent characteristics of the arrogant tyrants in every age [^1]. "Shaqi" (Wretched) refers to one who prepares the means of trouble, calamity, and punishment for himself; some have interpreted it as one who does not accept advice. It is evident that these two meanings are not separate from each other.

In a narration, we read that Prophet 'Isa عليه السلام says: "My heart is soft, and I consider myself small in my own eyes" (indicating that the opposites of 'Jabbar' and 'Shaqi' are these two attributes) [^2].

Finally, this infant (the Messiah) says: "And peace be upon me the day I was born, and the day I die, and the day I am raised alive" (وَ السَّلامُ عَلَيَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدْتُ وَ يَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَ يَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّا). As we said in the commentary on the verses related to Yahya عليه السلام, these three days in human life are three decisive and dangerous days where safety is not possible except by the grace of Allah. Therefore, this sentence appears both regarding Yahya عليه السلام and regarding the Messiah عليه السلام, with the difference that in the first case, Allah says these words, and in the second case, the Messiah عليه السلام makes this request.

Key Points

1- The Clearest Picture of the Birth of 'Isa (عليه السلام)

The eloquence and rhetoric of the Quran can be particularly understood in these important matters—how it presents a significant issue that had been mixed with so many superstitions in short, profound, vivid, content-rich, and completely expressive phrases, such that it separates and rejects every kind of superstition from it.

Interestingly, in the above verses, seven prominent attributes, two programs, and one supplication are mentioned. The seven attributes are: being a servant of Allah—mentioning this at the beginning of all attributes is an indication that the greatest station of man is the station of servitude ('ubudiyyah).

Following that is being the possessor of a Heavenly Book, and then the station of Prophethood (of course, we know that the station of Prophethood is not always accompanied by having a Heavenly Book).

Then, following the station of servitude and leadership, being "blessed"—meaning being useful to society—is mentioned. In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "The meaning of 'blessed' is 'naffa'' (meaning very beneficial)."

After that, being dutiful toward the mother is mentioned, and finally, not being an arrogant tyrant or wretched, and instead being humble, appreciative, and prosperous.

Among all programs, He emphasizes the Lord's recommendation of prayer and zakat. This is because of the extraordinary importance of these two programs, which are the secrets of connection with the Creator and the creation. In one sense, all religious programs can be summarized in them, as part of them defines man's bond with the creation and part with the Creator.

As for the supplication he makes for himself and the request he has from Allah at the beginning of his life, it is: "O Allah, keep me safe during these three days: the day of birth, the day of death, and the day I am raised in the Resurrection, and grant me security in these three sensitive stages!"

2- The Station of the Mother

Although Prophet 'Isa عليه السلام was born from a mother without a father by the decisive command of the Lord, the fact that we read from his tongue in the above verse, while enumerating his honors, the mention of being dutiful toward his mother, is clear evidence of the importance of the mother's station. Additionally, it shows that this newborn child, who spoke by a miracle, was aware of the fact that he is a unique child among humans, born only from a mother without the involvement of a father.

In any case, although much is said about the station of the mother in today's world and even a day has been dedicated as "Mother's Day," unfortunately, the state of machine civilization is such that it severs the relationship of fathers and mothers from children very early, so much so that fewer emotional ties are seen among them after they grow up.

In Islam, we have amazing narrations in this regard that recommend the extraordinary importance of the mother's station to Muslims, so that they strive in this matter in practice, not just in words. In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: A man came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said:

"O Messenger of Allah, to whom should I be kind? He said: Your mother. He said: Then who? He said: Your mother! He said: Then who? He said: Your mother! He said: Then who? He said: Your father!" [^1] In another hadith, we read: A young man came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to participate in Jihad (where Jihad was not an individual obligation). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"Do you have a mother? He said: Yes. He said: Stay with her, for Paradise is under her feet" [^1]. Undoubtedly, if we consider the immense hardships the mother endures from the time of conception to delivery, the period of breastfeeding, and until the child grows up—the pains, toils, sleeplessness, illnesses, and nursing that she accepts with open arms for her child—we will see that no matter how much a person strives in this path, he remains indebted to the rights of the mother.

Interestingly, in a hadith, we read: Umm Salama came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "All honors have gone to the men; what share do the poor women have of these honors?" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"Yes, when a woman becomes pregnant, she is in the position of one who fasts, stands in prayer, and strives with her life and wealth in the way of Allah. When she gives birth, Allah gives her such a reward that no one knows its extent due to its greatness. When she breastfeeds, for every suckle by the child, Allah gives her a reward equal to freeing a slave from the descendants of Isma'il. And when the period of breastfeeding ends, one of Allah's noble angels taps her side and says: 'Start your deeds anew, for Allah has forgiven all your sins!'" (As if your record of deeds begins fresh) [^1]. In volume 12 of Tafsir Nemouneh, under verse 23 of Surah Al-Isra, we had other discussions in this regard.

3- Parthenogenesis

Among the questions raised by the above verses is: Is the birth of a child without a father possible from a scientific perspective? Does the issue of 'Isa's عليه السلام birth from only a mother contradict scientific research in this field?

Undoubtedly, this matter occurred through a miracle, but modern science has not denied the possibility of such a thing; rather, it has explicitly stated its possibility. Particularly, the subject of parthenogenesis has been observed among many animals, and given that the issue of conception is not exclusive to humans, it proves the possibility of this matter in general.

Dr. Alexis Carrel, the famous French physiologist and biologist, writes in the book Man, The Unknown:

"When we think about the share each parent has in reproduction, we must remember the experiments of Loeb and Bataillon, showing that from an unfertilized frog egg, without the intervention of a spermatozoon, a new frog can be brought into existence through specific techniques. In this way, it is possible to substitute a chemical or physical factor for the 'male cell,' but in any case, the existence of a female factor is always necessary."

Therefore, what is scientifically certain for the birth of a child is the existence of the mother's ovum; otherwise, regarding the male sperm, another factor can substitute for it. For this reason, the issue of parthenogenesis is a reality accepted by doctors in today's world, even though it happens very rarely.

Beyond this, this matter in the face of the laws of creation and the power of Allah is as the Quran says: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسى‌ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ‌: "Indeed, the likeness of 'Isa with Allah is as the likeness of Adam; He created him from dust, then said to him, 'Be,' and he was [a complete being]" (Al-Imran, 59). That is, this miracle is not more significant than that miracle.

4- How a Newborn Speaks

It goes without saying that according to the normal course of things, no newborn speaks in the first hours or days of birth. Speaking requires sufficient brain development, followed by the training of the tongue and larynx muscles and the coordination of various body systems with one another; these matters usually take months to gradually develop in children.

However, we have no scientific evidence for the impossibility of this matter; it is merely a miracle, and all miracles are such—meaning they are extraordinary occurrences, not intellectual impossibilities. We have provided an explanation of this subject in the discussion on the miracles of the Prophets.

Ayahs

ذَٰلِكَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۚ قَوْلَ ٱلْحَقِّ ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ (٣٤)
That is Jesus, son of Mary, a Word of the Real concerning whom they are in doubt. (34)
مَا كَانَ لِلَّهِ أَن يَتَّخِذَ مِن وَلَدٍ ۖ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥٓ ۚ إِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (٣٥)
It is not for Allah to take a son. Immaculate is He! When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is. (35)

Commentary

Is a Son Possible for Allah?!

After the Holy Quran, in the preceding verses, presented a very vivid and clear depiction of the story of the birth of Prophet Christ عليه السلام, it proceeds to refute the superstitions and polytheistic claims made about Jesus, stating: "That is Jesus, the son of Mary" (ذلِكَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ‌).

In this expression, it specifically emphasizes his being the "son of Mary" to serve as a preamble for denying his being the "son of God."

It then adds: "This is a statement of truth about which they dispute" (قَوْلَ الْحَقِّ الَّذِي فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ‌) [1]. This phrase is, in fact, an affirmation of the absolute correctness of all the preceding accounts regarding Prophet Christ عليه السلام, asserting that there is not the slightest deviation from the truth in them.

As for the Quran stating that they are in doubt and dispute regarding this matter, it likely refers to the friends and enemies of Christ عليه السلام, or in other words, the Christians and the Jews. On one hand, a misguided group doubted the purity of his mother, and on the other hand, another group expressed doubt regarding his being a mere human. Even this latter group split into various sects; some explicitly considered him the son of God (a spiritual and physical son, literal and not metaphorical!!), subsequently giving rise to the doctrine of the Trinity and the three gods.

Some found the issue of the Trinity incomprehensible to the intellect and believed it must be accepted through blind faith, while others resorted to baseless arguments to provide a logical justification for it. In short, "since they did not see the truth—or did not wish to see it—they struck the path of myth" [2]!

In the following verse, it explicitly states: "It is not befitting for Allah to take a son; Glory be to Him!" (ما كانَ لِلَّهِ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ مِنْ وَلَدٍ سُبْحانَهُ‌).

[1]: Commentators have discussed the syntax of this sentence extensively, but what appears most correct linguistically and in light of the preceding verses is that "قَوْلَ الْحَقِّ" (The Statement of Truth) is the object (maf'ul) of an omitted verb, and "الَّذِي فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ" is its attribute. The implied meaning is: "I speak the statement of truth about which they dispute."

[2]: For further explanation regarding the Christian Trinity and the superstitions woven around it, refer to Volume 4 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 244, under verse 171 of Surah An-Nisa.

"...Rather, when He decrees a matter, He only says to it, 'Be,' and it is" (إِذا قَضى‌ أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ‌).

This points to the fact that having a son—as the Christians imagine regarding God—is incompatible with the sanctity of the Lord's station. On one hand, it necessitates being a physical body; on the other, it implies limitation; and on a third, it implies need. In short, it drags God from His holy station down under the umbrella of the laws of the material world and places Him at the level of a weak and limited material being.

The God who possesses such power and ability that if He wills, thousands of worlds like this vast universe we inhabit would come into existence with a single command and gesture—is it not polytheism and a deviation from the principles of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the knowledge of God to consider Him like a human who has a son, and a son who is of the same rank as the father and his equal?! The expression "كُنْ فَيَكُونُ" (Be, and it is), which appears in eight instances in the Quranic verses, is a very vivid depiction of the vastness of God's power and His authority and sovereignty over the matter of creation. No command can be imagined shorter than "Kun" (Be), and no result appears more expansive and comprehensive than "Fayakun" (and it is), especially considering the "Fa" of sequence (fa al-ta'qib) which denotes immediacy here. Indeed, as philosophers state, the "Fa" here does not signify a temporal delay, but rather a rank-based sequence, meaning the following of the effect upon the cause (reflect on this).

Denying a Son Means Denying Any Kind of Need in Allah.

Fundamentally, why do living beings need children? Is it not because their lifespan is limited, and so that their lineage does not become extinct and their species' life continues, children must be born from them?

From a social perspective, the need for collective work and more human labor causes man to have an interest in children.

Furthermore, emotional and psychological needs and the desire to eliminate the dread of loneliness invite him to this.

But are these matters conceivable regarding the God who is Eternal and Everlasting, whose power is infinite, and for whom the issue of emotional or other needs has no path to His Pure Essence?

Is it not the case that those who attributed a son to God measured Him by the scale of their own existence and saw in Him what they saw in themselves, while "There is nothing like unto Him" (لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْ‌ءٌ)? [1]

An Important Historical Point Regarding the First Migration

The first migration that occurred in Islam was the migration of a significant group of Muslims, both men and women, to the land of Abyssinia (Habasha). They left Mecca for Abyssinia to escape the clutches of the polytheists of Quraysh and to organize and prepare themselves further for future Islamic programs. As they had anticipated, they were able to live there in peace and engage in Islamic programs and self-purification.

This news reached the leaders of Quraysh in Mecca. They viewed this matter as an alarm bell for themselves and felt that "Abyssinia" would be a refuge for the Muslims, and perhaps after gaining strength and power, they would return to Mecca and create great problems for them.

After consultation, they decided to choose two active men of Quraysh to send to the "Negus" (Najashi), to explain the dangers of the Muslims' presence there to him and to have them expelled from that secure and peaceful land.

They dispatched "Amr ibn al-Aas" and "Abdullah ibn Abi Rabi'ah" with many gifts for the Negus and his high-ranking military commanders.

"Umm Salama," the wife of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, says: When we entered the land of Abyssinia, we were met with the good conduct of the Negus. We had no religious restrictions, and no one harassed us. However, the Quraysh, after learning of this, sent two men with many gifts and instructed them to meet with the high commanders before meeting the Negus himself and give them their gifts. Then, they were to present the gifts to the Negus and request that he surrender the Muslims to them without even speaking to them! They executed this plan precisely and told the Negus's commanders beforehand: "A group of foolish youths have taken refuge in your land; they have distanced themselves from their own religion and have not entered your religion either. They have innovated a new religion unknown to us and to you. The nobles of Quraysh have sent us to you so that we may rid this country of their evil and return them to their own people."

They obtained a promise from the commanders that whenever the Negus consulted them, they would support this view and say that their own people are more aware of their condition.

Then they were granted an audience with the Negus and spoke their deceptive words.

[1]: Regarding the meaning of "كُنْ فَيَكُونُ" and the proofs for denying a son to God, we have also discussed this in Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verses 116 and 117 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

This plan was progressing well, and these deceptive words, along with the many gifts, caused the Negus's entourage to confirm their claims.

Suddenly, the tide turned. The Negus became very angry and said: "By God, I will not do such a thing! These are a people who have sought refuge with me and have preferred my country over others because of its security. I will never act upon your proposal until I summon them and investigate."

"If it is truly as these men say, I will hand them over to these two and expel them; otherwise, they shall live well under the protection of my kindness."

"Umm Salama" says: The Negus sent for the Muslims. They consulted one another on what to say. Their decision was to tell the reality of the matter and explain the instructions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the program of Islam, come what may!

The day appointed for this summons was an extraordinary day. Christian elders and scholars, holding their holy books, had been invited to this assembly.

The Negus turned to the Muslims and asked: "What is this religion for which you have separated from your people and yet have not entered our faith?"

Ja'far ibn Abi Talib began to speak and said: "O King! We were a people living in ignorance (Jahiliyyah) and heedlessness. We worshipped idols, ate the meat of dead animals, committed shameful and disgraceful acts, treated our kin poorly and our neighbors badly; the strong devoured the weak. In short, our misery was great, until Allah raised a Prophet from among us whose lineage we knew well, and in whose truthfulness, trustworthiness, and purity we believed. He invited us to the One God and commanded us to set aside the worship of stones and wood that our ancestors practiced. He encouraged us to be truthful, to fulfill trusts, to maintain family ties, to be good to neighbors, and he forbade us from prohibitions, bloodshed, disgraceful acts, false testimony, consuming the property of orphans, and slandering chaste women.

He also commanded us to worship the One God, not to associate anything with Him, to establish prayer, to fast, and to pay the zakat..."

"We believed in him and followed his instructions to the letter. But our people transgressed against us, persecuted and harassed us, and insisted that we return from the religion of Monotheism to polytheism, and submit to the same former pollutions."

"When they pressured us from every side, we came to your country and wished to be your neighbors, hoping that no one here would wrong us!"

The Negus fell into deep thought. He turned to Ja'far and said: "Do you remember anything from the Heavenly Book of this man?" Ja'far said: "Yes." The Negus said: "Read it to me!" Ja'far, who possessed extraordinary intelligence, wit, and faith, chose the most appropriate passage of the Quran—these very opening verses of Surah Maryam—and recited them for the Negus and all those present who followed the Christian faith: "كهيعص ذِكْرُ رَحْمَتِ رَبِّكَ عَبْدَهُ زَكَرِيَّا ... وَ اذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ مَرْيَمَ إِذِ انْتَبَذَتْ مِنْ أَهْلِها مَكاناً شَرْقِيًّا ..."

When Ja'far recited these verses with a captivating tone and from the sincerity of his heart, it had such an effect on the soul of the Negus and the great Christian scholars that tears involuntarily flowed from their eyes and rolled down their cheeks.

The Negus turned to them and said: "By God, what Jesus Christ brought and these verses both spring from the same source of light. Go and live in peace and comfort; by God, I will never hand you over to these two men."

Later, the envoys of Quraysh made other attempts to prejudice the Negus against the Muslims, but it had no effect on his awakened soul. They returned from there disappointed and hopeless. He returned their gifts to them and dismissed them. [1]

[1]: Adapted from Sirat Ibn Hisham, Volume 1, pages 356-361.

Ayahs

وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ (٣٦)
[And Jesus said,] ‘Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord. So worship Him. This is a straight path.’ (36)
فَٱخْتَلَفَ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ مِنۢ بَيْنِهِمْ ۖ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن مَّشْهَدِ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (٣٧)
But the factions differed among themselves. So woe to the faithless at the scene of a tremendous day. (37)
أَسْمِعْ بِهِمْ وَأَبْصِرْ يَوْمَ يَأْتُونَنَا ۖ لَـٰكِنِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ ٱلْيَوْمَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣٨)
How well they will hear and how well they will see on the day when they come to Us! But today the wrongdoers are in manifest error. (38)
وَأَنذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْحَسْرَةِ إِذْ قُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ وَهُمْ فِى غَفْلَةٍ وَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٣٩)
Warn them of the Day of Regret, when the matter will be decided, while they are [yet] heedless and do not have faith. (39)
إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَرِثُ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَنْ عَلَيْهَا وَإِلَيْنَا يُرْجَعُونَ (٤٠)
Indeed We shall inherit the earth and whoever there is on it, and to Us they shall be brought back. (40)

Commentary

Resurrection, the Day of Regret and Sorrow

The final statement of Jesus (peace be upon him), after introducing himself with the aforementioned attributes, is an emphasis on the principle of Monotheism (Tawhid), specifically regarding worship. He says: "Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. This is the straight path" (وَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ رَبِّي وَ رَبُّكُمْ فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُونِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّي وَ رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ) [1]. In this way, Christ (peace be upon him) fought against every form of polytheism and the worship of dual or multiple gods from the very beginning of his life, emphasizing Monotheism everywhere. Therefore, what is seen as the Trinity (three gods) among Christians today is certainly an innovation (bid'ah) introduced after Jesus, the details of which we explained under the commentary of verse 171 of Surah An-Nisa [2]. Although some commentators have suggested the possibility that this sentence was spoken by the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family)—meaning that Allah commands him in this verse to invite people to Monotheism in worship and introduce it as the straight path.

However, other verses of the Quran testify that this sentence is the speech of Prophet Christ (peace be upon him) and a continuation of his previous words. In Surah Az-Zukhruf, verses 63 to 64, we read: وَ لَمَّا جاءَ عِيسى‌ بِالْبَيِّناتِ قالَ قَدْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَ لِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُونِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّي وَ رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ: "And when Jesus brought clear proofs, he said, 'I have come to you with wisdom and to make clear to you some of that over which you differ. So fear Allah and obey me. Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. This is the straight path.'"

Here, we see almost the exact same sentence quoted from the tongue of Jesus (a similar theme also appears in Surah Al-Imran, verses 50 and 51).

Despite all the emphasis Christ (peace be upon him) placed on Monotheism and the worship of the One God, "the factions from among them differed" (and expressed various beliefs, especially regarding Christ) (فَاخْتَلَفَ الْأَحْزابُ مِنْ بَيْنِهِمْ). "Woe to those who disbelieved (and took the path of polytheism) from the scene of a tremendous Day" (فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ مَشْهَدِ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ).

The history of Christianity also clearly bears witness to the extent they differed regarding him and the issue of Monotheism after Prophet Christ (peace be upon him). These differences escalated to such a degree that Constantine, the Roman Emperor, convened a council of "Bishops" (great Christian scholars), which is one of the three famous historical councils. The members of this council reached two thousand one hundred and seventy, all of whom were their prominent figures. When the discussion about Jesus was raised, the scholars present expressed completely different views about him, and each group held a specific belief.

Some said: He is God who descended to earth! He brought some to life and caused others to die, then ascended to heaven!

Others said: He is the Son of God!

And others said: He is one of the three persons (three holy essences): the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit!

And others said: He is the third of the three: Allah is a deity, he is a deity, and his mother is also a deity!

Finally, some said: He is the servant of Allah and His messenger.

Other sects each said something different, such that no consensus was reached on any of these beliefs. The largest number of supporters for a single belief was 308 people, which the Emperor accepted as a relative majority and defended as the official creed, discarding the rest. However, the belief in Monotheism, which unfortunately had fewer supporters, remained in the minority [1]. Since deviating from the principle of Monotheism is considered the greatest deviation of the Christians, we saw in the verse above how Allah threatens them that on the Great Day of Resurrection, in that public gathering and before the court of Divine Justice, they will have a grim and painful fate [2].

The next verse describes their state on the scene of Resurrection and says: "How [clearly] they will hear and see the Day they come to Us! But the wrongdoers today (in this world) are in manifest error" (أَسْمِعْ بِهِمْ وَ أَبْصِرْ يَوْمَ يَأْتُونَنا لكِنِ الظَّالِمُونَ الْيَوْمَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ).

It is clear that in the realm of the Hereafter, the veils are removed from the eyes, and ears become hearing, because the signs of Truth there are far more manifest than in the world. Fundamentally, witnessing that court and the effects of deeds removes the sleep of negligence from human eyes and ears, and even the blind-hearted become aware and knowledgeable. But what use is it, for this awakening and awareness is not beneficial to them.

Some commentators have taken the word "today" (al-yawm) in the phrase "But the wrongdoers today are in manifest error" to mean the Day of Judgment, making the meaning of the verse: "There they will become seeing and hearing, but this sight and hearing on that day will not benefit them, and they will be in manifest error."

However, the first interpretation seems more correct [1].

Once again, emphasizing the fate of the faithless and the wrongdoers on that day, He says: "And warn them of the Day of Regret (the Day of Resurrection) when the matter will be concluded while they are in heedlessness and they do not believe" (وَ أَنْذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ الْحَسْرَةِ إِذْ قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ وَ هُمْ فِي غَفْلَةٍ وَ هُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ). We know that the Day of Judgment has various names in the Holy Quran, including "the Day of Regret" (Yawm al-Hasrah). Both the righteous will feel regret—wishing they had performed more good deeds—and the evildoers, because the veils are removed and the realities of deeds and their results become manifest to everyone.

The phrase "when the matter is concluded" (إِذْ قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ) has been interpreted by some as referring to the completion of the programs of reckoning, reward, and obligation on the Day of Resurrection. Others consider it an allusion to the annihilation of the world; according to this interpretation, the meaning of the verse would be: "Warn them of the Day of Regret, that time when the world ends while they are in a state of heedlessness and lack of faith." (However, the first interpretation seems more correct, especially since in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the interpretation of the phrase "when the matter is concluded," it is quoted: "Meaning Allah decrees eternal life for the people of Paradise in it, and decrees eternal life for the people of Hell in it") [1].

The last verse under discussion warns all oppressors and wrongdoers that these possessions in their hands are not eternal, just as their own lives are not eternal. Rather, the ultimate heir of all these is Allah. He says: "Indeed, it is We who will inherit the earth and whoever is upon it, and to Us they will be returned" (إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَرِثُ الْأَرْضَ وَ مَنْ عَلَيْها وَ إِلَيْنا يُرْجَعُونَ) [2].

In reality, this verse is equivalent to verse 16 of Surah Al-Mu'min (Ghafir), which says: "To whom belongs the sovereignty this Day? To Allah, the One, the Prevailing" (لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ).

If one truly believes and is convinced of this reality, why would they permit transgression, oppression, and the trampling of truth or the rights of others for the sake of wealth and other material blessings that are entrusted to us for a few days and quickly pass from our hands?

[^2]: "Mashhad" in the above verse might be a mim-infinitive meaning "witnessing," or a noun of place or time meaning the place or time of witnessing. Although these meanings differ, they do not vary much in terms of the final result. (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 13, p. 72) [^1]: The "Alif and Lam" in "Al-Yawm" is the "Alif and Lam of identification" (al-ahd). According to the first interpretation, it is "present identification" (al-ahd al-huduri), and according to the second, it is "mention-based identification" (al-ahd al-dhikri). [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the aforementioned verse. [^2]: Does this verse refer to the Resurrection or the time of the world's annihilation? If it refers to the Resurrection, it seemingly does not align with the phrase "and to Us they will be returned." If it refers to the time of the world's annihilation, it does not fit with the phrase "and whoever is upon it," because at the end of the world, no one is alive on earth for the expression "whoever is upon it" to be accurate. Perhaps for this reason, some commentators like Allamah Tabataba'i interpret the sentence as: "Indeed, We inherit the earth from them." However, this interpretation is also slightly contrary to the literal appearance, as "whoever is upon it" is joined with a conjunction. Another possibility here is that the object of the verb "inherit" (narith) is sometimes the person who leaves the property, as in "And Solomon inherited David," and sometimes the property left behind, as in "We inherit the earth." In the verse above, both expressions are used.

Ayahs

وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صِدِّيقًا نَّبِيًّا (٤١)
And mention in the Book Abraham. Indeed, he was a truthful man and a prophet. (41)
إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ لِمَ تَعْبُدُ مَا لَا يَسْمَعُ وَلَا يُبْصِرُ وَلَا يُغْنِى عَنكَ شَيْـًٔا (٤٢)
When he said to his father, ‘Father! Why do you worship that which neither hears nor sees, and is of no avail to you in any way? (42)
يَـٰٓأَبَتِ إِنِّى قَدْ جَآءَنِى مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ مَا لَمْ يَأْتِكَ فَٱتَّبِعْنِىٓ أَهْدِكَ صِرَٰطًا سَوِيًّا (٤٣)
Father! Indeed a knowledge has already come to me, which has not come to you. So follow me that I may guide you to a right path. (43)
يَـٰٓأَبَتِ لَا تَعْبُدِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ كَانَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَصِيًّا (٤٤)
Father! Do not worship Satan. Indeed Satan is disobedient to the All-beneficent. (44)
يَـٰٓأَبَتِ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يَمَسَّكَ عَذَابٌ مِّنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ فَتَكُونَ لِلشَّيْطَـٰنِ وَلِيًّا (٤٥)
Father! I am indeed afraid that a punishment from the All-beneficent will befall you, and you will become Satan’s accomplice.’ (45)

Commentary

The Captivating and Crushing Logic of Ibrahim (عليه السلام)

The account of Prophet Masih (عليه السلام) regarding his birth, along with a portion of the life of his mother Maryam, has concluded. Following that, the verses under discussion and the upcoming verses unveil a part of the life of the hero of monotheism, Ibrahim al-Khalil, emphasizing that the call of this great Prophet—like all Divine leaders—began from the point of monotheism.

In the first verse, it says: "And mention in the Book (the Quran) Ibrahim" (وَ اذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ إِبْراهِيمَ‌).

"For he was a man of truth (a Siddiq), a Prophet" (إِنَّهُ كانَ صِدِّيقاً نَبِيًّا).

The word "Siddiq" is an intensive form of Sidq (truthfulness) and means one who is extremely truthful. Some have said it means one who never lies, or even higher, one who lacks the capacity to lie because they have habituated themselves to truthfulness throughout their entire life. Others consider it to mean one whose actions confirm his words and beliefs.

However, it is clear that all these meanings essentially return to a single concept.

In any case, this attribute is so significant that in the above verse, it is mentioned even before the attribute of "Prophethood" (Nubuwwah), as if it is the foundational merit for accepting Prophethood. Furthermore, the most prominent attribute required in Prophets and carriers of Divine revelation is this very meaning—that they convey the command of the Lord to the servants of Allah without any addition or subtraction.

Then, it proceeds to describe his conversation with his father "Azar" (father here refers to his uncle, and the word "Ab", as we have previously stated, in the Arabic language sometimes means father and sometimes uncle) [1] and says: "When he said to his father: O my father! Why do you worship that which hears not, sees not, and can avail you nothing?" (إِذْ قالَ لِأَبِيهِ يا أَبَتِ لِمَ تَعْبُدُ ما لا يَسْمَعُ وَ لا يُبْصِرُ وَ لا يُغْنِي عَنْكَ شَيْئاً).

This short and crushing statement is one of the best proofs for the negation of polytheism and idol-worship, because one of the human motivations regarding the recognition of the Lord is the motivation of profit and loss, which scholars of theology have expressed as the issue of "repelling potential harm."

He says: Why do you go toward a deity that not only fails to solve any of your problems but lacks the power of hearing and sight altogether?

In other words: Worship must be for One who has the power to solve problems, and beyond that, the Worshipped perceives the worshiper and his needs, being All-Hearing and All-Seeing; however, these idols lack all of these.

In reality, Ibrahim begins his call here with his father because influencing close relatives is more necessary, just as the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) was first commanded to invite his close kinsmen to Islam, as we read in verse 214 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara: "And warn your closest kindred" (وَ أَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ‌).

After that, Ibrahim, with clear logic, invites him to follow him in this matter, saying: "O my father! Indeed, there has come to me of knowledge that which has not come to you..."

[^1]: This matter is discussed in detail in Volume 5, page 303, under verse 74 of Surah Al-An'am.

"...so follow me, and listen to my word" (يا أَبَتِ إِنِّي قَدْ جاءَنِي مِنَ الْعِلْمِ ما لَمْ يَأْتِكَ فَاتَّبِعْنِي‌).

"Follow me, I will guide you to a straight path" (أَهْدِكَ صِراطاً سَوِيًّا).

I have gained much awareness through the path of Divine revelation, and I can say with certainty that I will not tread the path of error, nor will I ever invite you to a path of error. I desire your prosperity and happiness; accept from me so that you may be successful and reach the intended destination by traversing this straight path.

Then, he joins this positive aspect with the negative aspect and the consequences that follow opposition to this call, saying: "O my father! Worship not Satan; indeed, Satan has been ever rebellious against the Most Merciful (Allah)" (يا أَبَتِ لا تَعْبُدِ الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّ الشَّيْطانَ كانَ لِلرَّحْمنِ عَصِيًّا).

It is evident that the meaning of worship here is not worship in the sense of performing prostration, prayer, or fasting for Satan, but rather it means obedience and following commands, which is itself considered a type of worship.

The meaning of worship and adoration is so broad that it even includes listening to someone's words with the intention of acting upon them, and likewise, recognizing someone's law is considered a type of worship and adoration of them.

It is narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله):

"Whoever listens to a speaker has worshipped him; if the speaker speaks from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, he has worshipped Allah, and if the speaker speaks from Iblis, he has worshipped Iblis!" [1]

In any case, Ibrahim wants to teach his father this reality: that a human being in life cannot be without a "line"—it is either the line of Allah and the straight path, or the line of the rebellious and misguided Satan. He must think correctly in this regard, make a decision for himself, and consider his own good and welfare, far from prejudices and blind imitations.

Once again, he draws his attention to the grim consequences of polytheism and idol-worship, saying: "O my father! Indeed, I fear lest a punishment from the Most Merciful should afflict you, and you should become a companion to Satan" (يا أَبَتِ إِنِّي أَخافُ أَنْ يَمَسَّكَ عَذابٌ مِنَ الرَّحْمنِ فَتَكُونَ لِلشَّيْطانِ وَلِيًّا).

Ibrahim's expression toward his uncle Azar here is very interesting. On one hand, he repeatedly addresses him with the phrase "Ya Abati" (O my father), which is a sign of politeness and respect. On the other hand, the phrase "an yamassaka" (lest it touch you) shows that Ibrahim is distressed and concerned about even the slightest discomfort reaching Azar. Thirdly, the expression "punishment from the Most Merciful" ('adhabun minar-Rahman) points to the fact that your situation, due to this polytheism and idol-worship, has reached a point where the God whose general mercy encompasses everyone becomes angry with you and punishes you—see what a terrifying thing you are doing.

And fourthly, this action of yours is one whose end result is being placed under the umbrella of Satan's guardianship (wilayah).

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 115 (Root: 'Abd).

Key Points

1- The Way to Influence Others

The manner of Ibrahim's conversation with Azar—who, according to narrations, was an idol-worshiper, idol-carver, and idol-seller, and was considered a major agent of corruption in the environment—shows us that to influence deviated individuals, before resorting to harshness, one must use logic—a logic mixed with respect, affection, and compassion, yet coupled with decisiveness. This is because a large group will submit to the truth through this method, although some will still show resistance against this approach, who of course are a separate case and must be dealt with differently.

2- The Reason for Following a Scholar

In the above verses, we read that Ibrahim invites Azar to follow him, even though his uncle was naturally much older than him in age and more prominent in that society. He mentions the reason as: "I have knowledge which has not come to you" (قَدْ جاءَنِي مِنَ الْعِلْمِ ما لَمْ يَأْتِكَ‌).

This is a general law for everyone: that in what they do not know, they should follow those who do know. This, in fact, defines the program of referring to specialists in every field, including the issue of Taqlid (emulation) of a Mujtahid in the secondary branches of Islamic rulings. Of course, Ibrahim's discussion was not about matters related to the branches of religion, but rather he spoke of the most fundamental issues of the principles of religion; however, even in such matters, one must use the guidance of the knowledgeable so that guidance to the "even path," which is the "straight path," is achieved.

3- The Surah of Mercy and Remembrance

In this Surah, the phrase "Udhkur" (Mention/Remember) appears five times at the beginning of the stories of the great Prophets and Maryam. Because of this, this Surah can be called the "Surah of Remembrances"—remembrance of Prophets and great men and women, their monotheistic movement, and their efforts in the path of struggling against polytheism, idol-worship, oppression, and injustice.

And since "mention" (dhikr) usually means remembrance after forgetting, it may also point to the reality that the root of monotheism, the love for men of truth, and faith in their truth-seeking struggles are rooted in the depths of every human soul, and speaking of them is actually a type of mention, remembrance, and retelling.

Furthermore, the description of Allah as "Ar-Rahman" (The Most Merciful) appears sixteen times in this Surah. This is because the Surah begins with mercy—Allah's mercy to Zakariyya, Allah's mercy to Maryam and Masih—and its end is also with this same mercy, as it says near the end:

"Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds, the Most Merciful will appoint for them affection (in the hearts of the servants)" (Surah Maryam, Verse 96).

Ayahs

قَالَ أَرَاغِبٌ أَنتَ عَنْ ءَالِهَتِى يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ ۖ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ لَأَرْجُمَنَّكَ ۖ وَٱهْجُرْنِى مَلِيًّا (٤٦)
He said, ‘Abraham! Are you renouncing my gods? If you do not desist, I will stone you. Get away from me for a long while.’ (46)
قَالَ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكَ ۖ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّىٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِى حَفِيًّا (٤٧)
He said, ‘Peace be to you! I shall plead with my Lord to forgive you. Indeed, He is gracious to me. (47)
وَأَعْتَزِلُكُمْ وَمَا تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَأَدْعُوا۟ رَبِّى عَسَىٰٓ أَلَّآ أَكُونَ بِدُعَآءِ رَبِّى شَقِيًّا (٤٨)
I dissociate myself from you and whatever you invoke besides Allah. I will supplicate my Lord. Hopefully, I will not be disappointed in supplicating my Lord.’ (48)
فَلَمَّا ٱعْتَزَلَهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۖ وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنَا نَبِيًّا (٤٩)
So when he had left them and what they worshipped besides Allah, We gave him Isaac and Jacob, and each We made a prophet. (49)
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُم مِّن رَّحْمَتِنَا وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا (٥٠)
And We gave them out of Our mercy, and conferred on them a worthy and lofty repute. (50)

Commentary

The Result of Distancing from Shirk and Idolaters

In the preceding verses, the logical arguments of Ibrahim, infused with a particular grace and affection, were presented in the path of guiding his father. Now, it is time to recount the responses of Azar, so that by comparing the two, the truth and reality may become manifest and clear.

The Quran states: Not only did Ibrahim’s compassion and his profound discourse fail to touch Azar’s heart, but he became severely enraged upon hearing these words and "said: 'O Ibrahim, are you turning away from my gods?'" (قالَ أَ راغِبٌ أَنْتَ عَنْ آلِهَتِي يا إِبْراهِيمُ‌).

"If you do not desist, I will surely stone you" (لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهِ لَأَرْجُمَنَّكَ‌).

"And now, depart from me for a long time" (وَ اهْجُرْنِي مَلِيًّا).

It is noteworthy that, firstly, Azar was not even willing to utter expressions of denying the idols or opposing and speaking ill of them; rather, he only said: "Are you turning away from the idols?" lest the idols be insulted. Secondly, when threatening Ibrahim, he threatened him with stoning, and that too with the emphasis derived from the "Lam" and the "Nun of heavy emphasis" in "La-arjumannaka" (لارجمنك); and we know that stoning is one of the worst forms of execution. Thirdly, he did not settle for this conditional threat, but at that very moment, he considered Ibrahim’s presence unbearable and told him to stay away from his sight forever (the word maliyyan, according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, comes from the root imla', meaning to grant a long respite, and here its meaning is to stay away from me for a long time or forever).

This is a very insulting expression used by harsh individuals toward their opponents, and in Persian, we sometimes say "get lost" (lose your grave) instead; meaning not only should you hide yourself from me forever, but go somewhere where I won't even see your grave!

Some commentators have not taken the phrase "La-arjumannaka" to mean stoning, but rather interpreted it as verbal abuse or accusation. However, this interpretation seems unlikely; an examination of other Quranic verses where this same expression appears testifies to what we have stated.

Yet, despite all this, Ibrahim, like all prophets and heavenly leaders, maintained his composure. In the face of this extreme harshness and violence, he "said: 'Peace be upon you'" (قالَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكَ‌) with the utmost nobility.

This "Salam" might be a valediction and farewell, by saying which—and the few sentences following it—Ibrahim left Azar. It could also be a "Salam" spoken to end a dispute, as we read in Surah Al-Qasas, verse 55: لَنا أَعْمالُنا وَ لَكُمْ أَعْمالُكُمْ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ لا نَبْتَغِي الْجاهِلِينَ‌: "Now that you do not accept from us, our deeds are for us and your deeds are for you; peace be upon you, we do not seek the ignorant."

Then he added: "I will soon ask forgiveness for you from my Lord, for He is ever Gracious and Kind to me" (سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي إِنَّهُ كانَ بِي حَفِيًّا).

In fact, Ibrahim responded to Azar’s violence and threats with the opposite; he promised him seeking of forgiveness and a request for the Lord's pardon.

Here a question arises: Why did Ibrahim promise him forgiveness when we know that Azar never believed, and seeking forgiveness for polytheists is forbidden according to the explicit text of Surah At-Tawbah, verse 113?

We have provided a detailed answer to this question under that same verse of Surah At-Tawbah (Volume 8, page 106).

Then he said: "I shall withdraw from you (from you and this idol-worshipping people) and from what you call upon besides Allah"—meaning the idols (وَ أَعْتَزِلُكُمْ وَ ما تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ‌).

"And I will call upon my Lord; I hope that I will not be unblessed in calling upon my Lord" (وَ أَدْعُوا رَبِّي عَسى‌ أَلَّا أَكُونَ بِدُعاءِ رَبِّي شَقِيًّا).

This verse, on one hand, shows Ibrahim’s etiquette toward Azar—when he said "depart from me," Ibrahim accepted—and on the other hand, it defines his decisiveness in his belief: that my distancing from you is not because I have abandoned my firm belief in Monotheism, but because of your lack of readiness to accept the truth, and therefore I remain steadfast in my belief.

Furthermore, he says: If I call upon my Lord, He answers; but woe to you who call upon those more helpless than yourselves, whose prayers are never answered and who do not even hear your words.

Ibrahim fulfilled his word and remained with the utmost steadfastness in his belief. He was always the herald of Monotheism, even though the entire corrupt society of that day rose against him. However, he did not remain alone in the end; he gained numerous followers throughout all centuries and ages, such that all monotheists in the world take pride in his existence.

The Quran says in this regard: "So when he had withdrawn from them and from what they worshipped besides Allah, We bestowed upon him Ishaq (Isaac) and after Ishaq, his son Ya'qub (Jacob), and each of them We made a great prophet" (فَلَمَّا اعْتَزَلَهُمْ وَ ما يَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَ يَعْقُوبَ وَ كُلًّا جَعَلْنا نَبِيًّا).

Although it took a long time before Allah gave Ishaq and then Ya'qub (the son of Ishaq) to Ibrahim, nevertheless, this great blessing—a son like Ishaq and a grandson like Ya'qub, each of whom was a high-ranking prophet—was the result of that steadfastness Ibrahim عليه السلام showed in the path of fighting idols and withdrawing from that false religion.

In addition, "We bestowed upon them of Our mercy" (وَ وَهَبْنا لَهُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِنا). A special mercy reserved for the sincere, the devoted, and the men who strive and fight in the way of Allah.

And finally, "We granted them a high honor and a truthful mention among all nations" (وَ جَعَلْنا لَهُمْ لِسانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا).

This is, in fact, a response to Ibrahim’s request mentioned in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verse 84: وَ اجْعَلْ لِي لِسانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ‌: "O Allah, grant me a truthful mention among the later generations."

In reality, they wanted Ibrahim and his progeny to be so rejected from human society that not the slightest trace or news of them would remain and they would be forgotten forever. But on the contrary, Allah, because of their sacrifices, selflessness, and steadfastness in fulfilling the mission they were charged with, made them so renowned that they have always been and continue to be on the tongues of the people of the world, recognized as a model and pattern of God-consciousness, struggle, purity, piety, and jihad.

"Lisan" (tongue) in such contexts means the memory of a person among the people. When we add "Sidq" (truth) to it and say Lisan al-Sidq, it means a good mention, a good name, and a favorable memory among the people. And when it is joined with the word "Aliyya," which means high and prominent, its concept is that a very excellent memory of someone remains among the people.

It goes without saying that Ibrahim does not seek to satisfy a personal desire with this request; rather, his goal is that the enemies should not be able to consign his life story—which was exceptionally character-building—to oblivion, nor erase him, who can be a model for the people of the world, from memories forever.

In a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read: "A truthful mention (Lisan al-Sidq) that Allah grants a person among the people is better than abundant wealth which he both consumes himself and leaves as an inheritance." [^1] Fundamentally, regardless of spiritual aspects, sometimes a good reputation among the people can become a great asset for a person and his children, examples of which we have seen in abundance.

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi (as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 339).

Here a question arises: How is it that in this verse, the blessing of the existence of Ismail, Ibrahim’s noble firstborn son, is not mentioned at all, while the name of Ya'qub, Ibrahim’s grandson, is explicitly mentioned?

And elsewhere in the Quran, the existence of Ismail is mentioned among Ibrahim’s blessings, where it says from the tongue of Ibrahim: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْماعِيلَ وَ إِسْحاقَ‌: "Praise be to Allah, who has granted me in old age Ismail and Ishaq" (Ibrahim: 39).

The answer to this question is that: besides the fact that two or three verses later the name of Ismail and some of his prominent attributes are mentioned independently, the purpose of the above verse is to describe the continuation and succession of prophethood in Ibrahim’s lineage and to show how this good reputation, noble name, and great history were realized through the prophets who emerged from his lineage one after another. We know that many prophets emerged from the lineage of Ishaq and Ya'qub throughout the ages and centuries, even though from the lineage of Ismail also the greatest of prophets, namely the Prophet of Islam, stepped onto the stage of existence; however, the succession and continuity were in the children of Ishaq.

Therefore, in Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 27, we read: وَ وَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَ يَعْقُوبَ وَ جَعَلْنا فِي ذُرِّيَّتِهِ النُّبُوَّةَ وَ الْكِتابَ‌: "And We gave him Ishaq and Ya'qub and placed in his progeny prophethood and the Scripture."

Ayahs

وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مُوسَىٰٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مُخْلَصًا وَكَانَ رَسُولًا نَّبِيًّا (٥١)
And mention in the Book Moses. Indeed he was exclusively dedicated [to Allah], and an apostle and prophet. (51)
وَنَـٰدَيْنَـٰهُ مِن جَانِبِ ٱلطُّورِ ٱلْأَيْمَنِ وَقَرَّبْنَـٰهُ نَجِيًّا (٥٢)
We called him from the right side of the Mount and We drew him near [to Ourselves] for confidential discourse. (52)
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥ مِن رَّحْمَتِنَآ أَخَاهُ هَـٰرُونَ نَبِيًّا (٥٣)
And We gave him out of Our mercy his brother Aaron, a prophet. (53)

Commentary

Moses, a Sincere and Chosen Prophet

The three aforementioned verses provide a brief reference to Moses (عليه السلام), who is a descendant of the lineage of Abraham and one of the blessings bestowed upon that great man, following and completing his path. First, addressing the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), Allah says: And mention Moses in the Book (وَ اذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ مُوسى‌).

Then, He recounts five types of blessings granted to this great Prophet:

1- Through his obedience and servitude to God, he reached a station where "his Lord made him pure and sincere" (إِنَّهُ كانَ مُخْلَصاً).

Undoubtedly, whoever reaches such a station will be immune to the danger of deviation and pollution. This is because Satan, despite all his persistence in leading the servants of Allah astray, himself admits that he has no power to mislead the "sincere ones" (mukhlasin): قالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ‌: "He said: 'By Your might, I will surely mislead them all, except Your sincere servants among them'" (Surah Sad, 82-83).

2- "He was a high-ranking Messenger and a Prophet" (وَ كانَ رَسُولًا نَبِيًّا).

The reality of Risalah (Messengership) is that a mission is entrusted to someone, and they are obligated to propagate and fulfill that mission; this is a station held by all prophets who were commanded to invite people.

The mention of Nabiyyan (Prophet) here refers to the loftiness of the station and the high rank of this great Prophet, as this word is originally derived from Nubuwah (on the weight of naghmah), meaning loftiness and high status. Of course, it has another root derived from Naba', meaning news, because a Prophet receives divine news and informs others. However, in this context, the first meaning is more appropriate.

3- The next verse refers to the beginning of Moses' mission, saying: "We called him from the right side of Mount Tur" (وَ نادَيْناهُ مِنْ جانِبِ الطُّورِ الْأَيْمَنِ‌). On that dark and terrifying night when he was passing through the deserts of Midian with his wife toward Egypt, his wife went into labor. He was caught in severe cold and was searching for a flame of fire when suddenly a light shone from afar, a voice arose, and Moses (عليه السلام) was given the command of Messengership. This was the greatest honor and the sweetest moment of his life.

4- Furthermore, "We brought him near and spoke with him" (وَ قَرَّبْناهُ نَجِيًّا). [^1] The divine call was one blessing, and speaking with Him was another.

And finally, "Out of Our mercy, We granted him his brother Aaron, who was also a prophet," to be his supporter, helper, and colleague (وَ وَهَبْنا لَهُ مِنْ رَحْمَتِنا أَخاهُ هارُونَ نَبِيًّا).

Key Points

1- Who is the "Mukhlas"?

In the verses above, we read that Allah placed Moses among His "Mukhlas" servants (with a fatha on the lam). As we mentioned, this is a very grand station—a station accompanied by divine insurance against slips and deviations. It is a station of invulnerability against Satan, which is achieved only under the shadow of continuous struggle against the self (jihad al-nafs) and persistent, dedicated obedience to Allah's command.

The masters of ethics consider this station to be very high and sublime. It is understood from the verses of the Quran that the "Mukhlasin" possess specific characteristics and honors, which will be discussed under the appropriate verses, God willing.

2- The Difference Between a Messenger (Rasul) and a Prophet (Nabi)

"Rasul" (Messenger) originally refers to someone who has been entrusted with a mission and a message to deliver. "Nabi" (Prophet), according to one interpretation, refers to someone who is aware of divine revelation and provides news of it, and according to another interpretation, refers to a person of high status (the roots of both were explained previously). This is from a linguistic perspective.

However, regarding Quranic terminology and the language of the Hadiths, some believe that a "Rasul" is one who possesses a divine law (sharia) and is commanded to propagate it—meaning he receives divine revelation and delivers it to the people. A "Nabi," however, receives revelation but is not obligated to propagate it; rather, it is for his own duty, or he answers if he is asked.

In other words, a "Nabi" is like a knowledgeable physician who is ready in his clinic to receive patients; he does not go after the patients, but if a patient refers to him, he does not withhold treatment.

But a "Rasul" is like a mobile physician. As Ali (عليه السلام) said in Nahj al-Balagha regarding the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله):

A physician who rotates with his medicine

[^1] He goes everywhere—to cities, villages, mountains, and deserts—to find the sick and treat them. He is a spring that runs after the thirsty, not a spring that the thirsty must search for.

From the narrations that have reached us on this subject, which the late Kulayni recorded in Usul al-Kafi in the chapters "Classes of Prophets and Messengers" and "The Difference Between the Prophet and the Messenger," it is understood that a "Nabi" is one who only sees the truths of revelation in a state of sleep (like the vision of Abraham) or, in addition to sleep, hears the voice of the angel of revelation while awake.

However, a "Rasul" is one who, in addition to receiving revelation in sleep and hearing the voice of the angel, also observes the angel himself. [^1] Of course, what is mentioned in these narrations does not contradict the interpretation we provided, as it is possible that the differing missions of the Prophet and the Messenger influence the manner in which they receive revelation. In other words, every stage of a mission is accompanied by a specific stage of revelation (reflect on this).

[^1]: "Najiy" in the sense of "Munaji" is someone who has a form of conversation (whispering/intimate discourse) with another. Here, Allah first called Moses from a distance and then, when he drew near, engaged in intimate discourse with him. (It goes without saying that Allah has neither a tongue nor a place; rather, He creates sound waves in space and speaks with a servant like Moses).

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 108.

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, pp. 133-134 (Dar al-Kutub al-Islamiyyah edition).

Ayahs

وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِسْمَـٰعِيلَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صَادِقَ ٱلْوَعْدِ وَكَانَ رَسُولًا نَّبِيًّا (٥٤)
And mention in the Book Ishmael. Indeed, he was true to his promise, and an apostle and prophet. (54)
وَكَانَ يَأْمُرُ أَهْلَهُۥ بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ وَكَانَ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦ مَرْضِيًّا (٥٥)
He used to bid his family to [maintain] the prayer and to [pay] the zakat, and was pleasing to his Lord. (55)

Commentary

Ishmael, the Prophet Truthful to His Promise

After mentioning Abraham and his sacrifices, as well as a brief reference to a segment of the life of Moses, the discourse turns to "Ishmael," the eldest son of Abraham. It completes the remembrance of Abraham with the remembrance of his son Ishmael, and his programs with Ishmael's programs, describing five of his prominent attributes that can serve as a model for everyone.

Addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "And mention in the Book, Ishmael" (وَ اذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ إِسْماعِيلَ‌).

"Indeed, he was truthful to his promise" (إِنَّهُ كانَ صادِقَ الْوَعْدِ).

"And he was a messenger, a prophet" (وَ كانَ رَسُولًا نَبِيًّا).

"He used to enjoin his family to prayer and zakat" (وَ كانَ يَأْمُرُ أَهْلَهُ بِالصَّلاةِ وَ الزَّكاةِ).

"And he was ever pleasing to his Lord" (وَ كانَ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِ مَرْضِيًّا).

In these two verses, being truthful to promises, being a high-ranking prophet, enjoining prayer and maintaining a link and relationship with the Creator, enjoining zakat and establishing a relationship with God's creation, and finally, performing deeds that attract the pleasure of God, are counted among the attributes of this great divine prophet.

The emphasis on fulfilling promises and the attention given to the upbringing of the family points to the extraordinary importance of these two divine duties; one is mentioned before his status of prophethood and the other immediately after it.

In reality, until a person is "truthful," it is impossible to reach the lofty station of messengership, because the first condition of this station is to convey the divine revelation to His servants without any addition or omission. Therefore, even the few individuals who deny the station of infallibility (Ismah) in some of its dimensions regarding the prophets have accepted the issue of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله truthfulness as an essential condition—truthfulness and honesty in news, in promises, and in everything.

In a narration, we read: The reason Allah described Ishmael as "truthful to his promise" is that he was so insistent on fulfilling his promise that he made an appointment with someone at a certain place, and when the person did not show up, Ishmael waited for him there for an entire year! When the person finally arrived after this period, Ishmael said: "I have been waiting for you all this time!" [1] It is obvious that this does not mean Ishmael suspended his work and life; rather, it means that while continuing his programs, he remained watchful for the arrival of

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, Volume 2, Page 86.

the said person.

Regarding the fulfillment of promises, we have discussed this in detail (in Volume 4, Page 242) under the first verse of Surah Al-Ma'idah.

On the other hand, the first stage for propagating the message is to start with one's own family, who are the closest people to a person. For this reason, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله also began his mission first with his noble wife Khadijah and his cousin Ali عليه السلام, and then, according to the command "And warn your closest kindred" (وَ أَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ) [1], he turned to his close relatives.

In verse 132 of Surah Ta-Ha, we also read: وَ أْمُرْ أَهْلَكَ بِالصَّلاةِ وَ اصْطَبِرْ عَلَيْها: "And enjoin prayer upon your family and be steadfast therein."

Another point worth mentioning here is that by describing Ishmael as "pleasing" (mardiyya), it actually points to the fact that he had gained God's satisfaction in all his programs. Fundamentally, there is no blessing higher than the Object of Worship, the Master, and the Creator of man being satisfied and pleased with him. For this reason, in verse 119 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, after describing the blessing of the eternal Paradise for the special servants of God, it says at the end: رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَ رَضُوا عَنْهُ ذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ‌: "Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him; that is the great success." [2]

[1]: Surah Ash-Shu'ara, Verse 214.

[2]: We have had a more detailed discussion on this matter in Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, Page 139.

Ayahs

وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِدْرِيسَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صِدِّيقًا نَّبِيًّا (٥٦)
And mention in the Book Idrees. Indeed, he was a truthful one and a prophet, (56)
وَرَفَعْنَـٰهُ مَكَانًا عَلِيًّا (٥٧)
and We raised him to an exalted station. (57)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مِن ذُرِّيَّةِ ءَادَمَ وَمِمَّنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّةِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَمِمَّنْ هَدَيْنَا وَٱجْتَبَيْنَآ ۚ إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ خَرُّوا۟ سُجَّدًا وَبُكِيًّا ۩ (٥٨)
They are the ones whom Allah has blessed from among the prophets of Adam’s progeny, and from [the progeny of] those We carried with Noah, and from among the progeny of Abraham and Israel, and from among those that We guided and chose. When the signs of the All-beneficent were recited to them, they would fall down weeping in prostration. (58)
۞ فَخَلَفَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضَاعُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلشَّهَوَٰتِ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا (٥٩)
But they were succeeded by an evil posterity who neglected the prayer, and followed [their base] appetites. So they will soon encounter [the reward of] perversity, (59)
إِلَّا مَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا (٦٠)
barring those who repent, believe, and act righteously. Such will enter paradise, and they will not be wronged in the least. (60)

Commentary

These were the true Prophets, but...

In the final part of the reminders in this Surah, mention is made of the Prophet Idris (peace be upon him).

First, it says: "And mention in the Book (the Quran) Idris; indeed, he was a man of truth (Siddiq) and a prophet" (وَ اذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ إِدْرِيسَ إِنَّهُ كانَ صِدِّيقاً نَبِيًّا).

"Siddiq," as we have previously mentioned, refers to a person who is intensely truthful, who confirms the signs of Allah, and who is entirely submissive to the truth and reality.

Then, referring to his high-ranking status, it says: "And We raised him to a high station" (وَ رَفَعْناهُ مَكاناً عَلِيًّا). There is discussion among commentators as to whether this refers to the greatness of Idris's spiritual station or the height of his physical location. Some, as we have chosen, consider it an allusion to the spiritual stations and divine degrees of this great Prophet. Others believe that Allah took Idris up to the heavens, similar to Jesus (peace be upon him), and they interpret the expression "a high station" (makanan 'aliyya) in the above verse as a reference to this.

However, the application of the word "station" (makan) to spiritual ranks is a common and standard usage.

In Surah Yusuf, verse 77, we read that Yusuf said to his brothers, who had committed a wrong: "You are worse in station" (أَنْتُمْ شَرٌّ مَكاناً), meaning "you are the worst of people in terms of rank and status."

In any case, Idris is one of the high-ranking Prophets of Allah, whose biography will be discussed in the "Key Points."

Then, as a summary of all the honors mentioned in the preceding verses regarding the great Prophets, their attributes, states, and the blessings Allah bestowed upon them, it says: "Those were the ones upon whom Allah bestowed favor from among the prophets" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ). "...of the descendants of Adam, and of those We carried [in the ship] with Noah, and of the descendants of Abraham and Israel" (مِنْ ذُرِّيَّةِ آدَمَ وَ مِمَّنْ حَمَلْنا مَعَ نُوحٍ وَ مِنْ ذُرِّيَّةِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ إِسْرائِيلَ).

Although all these Prophets were descendants of Adam, they are mentioned as "descendants of Abraham and Israel" due to their proximity to one of these great Prophets. Accordingly, the "descendants of Adam" in this verse refers to Idris, who, according to the famous view, was the forefather of the Prophet Noah. The "descendants of those We carried with Noah" refers to Abraham, as Abraham was among the descendants of Shem (the son of Noah).

The "descendants of Abraham" refers to Isaac, Ishmael, and Jacob; and the "descendants of Israel" refers to Moses, Aaron, Zechariah, John, and Jesus, whose states and many of whose outstanding attributes were mentioned in the preceding verses.

Then, it completes this discussion by mentioning the true followers of these great Prophets, saying: "And of those We guided and chose. When the verses of the Most Merciful were recited to them, they fell down prostrating and weeping" (وَ مِمَّنْ هَدَيْنا وَ اجْتَبَيْنا إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُ الرَّحْمنِ خَرُّوا سُجَّداً وَ بُكِيًّا). [1] Some commentators have considered the phrase "of those We guided and chose..." to be another description of the same Prophets mentioned at the beginning of the verse, but what we stated above seems closer to the truth. [2] Evidence for this is a Hadith narrated from Imam Zayn al-Abidin Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, who, upon reciting this verse, said: "We are the ones intended by it" (نحن عنينا بها). [3] It is obvious that this statement never implies exclusivity; rather, it identifies the clearest manifestation (misdaq) of the true followers of the Prophets. We have encountered examples of this many times in this Tafsir Nemouneh.

However, failure to pay attention to this reality has caused commentators like "Alusi" in Ruh al-Ma'ani to fall into error, mocking this Hadith and considering it evidence for the lack of credibility of Shia Hadiths! This is the result of a lack of awareness regarding the true meaning of narrations that have been transmitted concerning the interpretation of verses.

It is noteworthy that in the preceding verses, mention was made of "Mary," even though she was not a Prophet; she is among those included in the phrase "of those We guided."

[1] "Sujjad" is the plural of "Sajid" (one who prostrates), and "Bukiyya" is the plural of "Baki" (one who weeps).

[2] Because if it referred to the past Prophets, it would not be consistent with the present/future tense verb (tutla - "are recited"), unless we assume a word like "kanu" (they were) is implied, which is contrary to the apparent meaning.

[3] Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse in question.

And she is considered one of its manifestations. In every time and place, it has had and continues to have one or more manifestations.

For this reason, in Surah An-Nisa, verse 69, we also see that those who receive Allah's blessings are not limited to the Prophets; rather, the "truthful ones" (Siddiqin) and the "martyrs" (Shuhada) are also added (فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَ الصِّدِّيقِينَ وَ الشُّهَداءِ).

In Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 75, Mary, the mother of Jesus, is also described as a "Siddiqah" (truthful woman), where it says: "And his mother was a woman of truth" (وَ أُمُّهُ صِدِّيقَةٌ).

Then, it speaks of a group that separated themselves from the character-building school of the Prophets and turned out to be unworthy successors. It enumerates some of their evil deeds, saying: "But there came after them successors who neglected prayer and pursued desires; so they will soon meet perdition" (فَخَلَفَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضاعُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ اتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَواتِ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا). "Khalaf" (with a short 'a' sound like barf) refers to unworthy children, or so-called "unworthy successors," whereas "Khalaf" (with a different vowel structure like sadaf) refers to a righteous child.

This sentence may refer to a group of the Children of Israel who walked the path of misguidance, forgot Allah, preferred the pursuit of desires over the remembrance of Allah, filled the world with corruption, and finally met the consequences of their evil deeds in this world, as they will in the Hereafter.

Regarding what is meant by "neglecting prayer" (idha'at al-salat) here—whether it means abandoning prayer, delaying it from its proper time, or performing actions that cause prayer to be lost within society—commentators have offered various possibilities. However, the latter meaning seems more accurate.

Why, among all acts of worship, is prayer singled out here? Perhaps the reason is that "prayer," as we know, is a barrier between man and sins. When this barrier is broken, drowning in desires is the inevitable result. In other words, just as the Prophets began their elevation of status through the remembrance of Allah—falling prostrate and weeping when Allah's verses were recited to them—these unworthy successors began their fall and deviation by forgetting the remembrance of Allah.

Since the program of the Quran everywhere is to leave the path of return to faith and truth open, here too, after mentioning the fate of the unworthy generations, it says: "Except those who repent, believe and do righteous work; for those will enter Paradise and will not be wronged at all" (إِلَّا مَنْ تابَ وَ آمَنَ وَ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَأُولئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ وَ لا يُظْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً). Therefore, it is not the case that if someone drowns in desires for a day, the seal of despair and hopelessness regarding Allah's mercy is forever stamped upon their forehead. Rather, as long as a breath remains and man is in the life of this world, the path of return and repentance is open.

Key Points

Who is Idris?

According to the reports of many commentators, "Idris" is the great-grandfather of Noah. His name in the Torah is "Enoch" (Akhnukh) and in Arabic "Idris," which some believe is derived from the root dars (to study/teach), because he was the first person to write with a pen. In addition to the station of prophethood, he had mastery over the sciences of astronomy, mathematics, and mechanics, and was the first person to teach humans the art of sewing clothes.
