Regarding this great Prophet, the Quran speaks only twice—and even then with brief references—once in the verses under discussion and again in Surah Al-Anbiya, verses 85 and 86. Various narrations provide detailed accounts of his life, though not all can be considered authentic. For this reason, we content ourselves with the above brief mention and move on from this discussion.

  1. In a Hadith found in many books of Sunni scholars, we read that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited the verse "But there came after them successors..." he said:

"After sixty years, people will come to power who will neglect prayer and drown in desires, and they will soon see the result of their misguidance. Then there will be successors who recite the Quran, but it does not go beyond their collarbones. The Quran is read by three types: the believer, the hypocrite, and the wicked." [1] It is noteworthy that if we take the starting point of the sixty years to be the Hijrah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it corresponds exactly to the time when "Yazid" sat upon the throne of power and the Master of Martyrs, Imam Husayn عليه السلام, and his companions drank the nectar of martyrdom. After this came the remainder of the Umayyad and Abbasid eras, who were content with only the name of Islam and the mere words of the Quran. We seek refuge in Allah from being part of such an unworthy group.

[1] Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 14, p. 84.

Ayahs

جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ ٱلَّتِى وَعَدَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ عِبَادَهُۥ بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ وَعْدُهُۥ مَأْتِيًّا (٦١)
Gardens of Eden promised by the All-beneficent to His servants, [while they were still] unseen. Indeed His promise is bound to come to pass. (61)
لَّا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا إِلَّا سَلَـٰمًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ رِزْقُهُمْ فِيهَا بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا (٦٢)
Therein they will not hear vain talk, but only ‘Peace!’ Therein they will have their provision morning and evening. (62)
تِلْكَ ٱلْجَنَّةُ ٱلَّتِى نُورِثُ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا مَن كَانَ تَقِيًّا (٦٣)
This is the paradise We will give as inheritance to those of Our servants who are Godwary. (63)

Commentary

A Description of Paradise

In these verses, a description is given of Paradise and the heavenly blessings that were mentioned in the preceding verses.

First, it describes the promised Paradise as follows: "Gardens of eternity which the Most Compassionate has promised His servants in the unseen," (though they have not seen it, they believe in it) (جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدَ الرَّحْمنُ عِبادَهُ بِالْغَيْبِ‌).

"Indeed, His promise is ever to be fulfilled" (إِنَّهُ كانَ وَعْدُهُ مَأْتِيًّا).

It is noteworthy that in the previous verses, where the discussion was about repentance, faith, and righteous deeds followed by the promise of Paradise, the word "Garden" (Jannah) appeared in the singular; however, here it appears in the plural (Jannat). This is because Paradise is, in reality, composed of numerous gardens and extraordinarily bountiful estates placed at the disposal of the righteous believers.

The description "Adn" (عدن), which means perpetual and eternal, serves as proof that Paradise is not like the gardens and blessings of this world that are prone to vanish. What causes man anxiety regarding the great blessings of this world is that they are all ultimately perishable, but this concern does not exist regarding the blessings of Paradise. [^1] The word "His servants" (عباده) refers to the believing servants of Allah, not all servants. The expression "in the unseen" (بالغیب) mentioned afterward means it is hidden from their eyes yet they believe in it. We also read in Surah Al-Fajr (Verse 30): فَادْخُلِي فِي عِبادِي وَ ادْخُلِي جَنَّتِي‌: "Enter among My servants, and enter My Paradise!" There is also the possibility that "in the unseen" means the blessings of Paradise are such that no eye has seen, no ear has heard, and they have not even crossed the minds of humans; they are entirely absent from our senses and perception. It is a world superior, vaster, and beyond this world, of which we only see a shadow from afar with the eye of the soul.

After that, it points to another of the greatest blessings of Paradise, saying:

[^1]: "Adn" literally means residence, and here it conveys the concept that its inhabitants will be "resident" therein forever.

"They will not hear therein any ill speech" (لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيها لَغْواً).

No lies, no insults, no slanders, no stinging remarks, no mockery, and not even any vain talk.

"Only [greetings of] peace" (إِلَّا سَلاماً).

"Salam" in the broad sense of the word, which indicates the health and safety of the soul, thought, tongue, behavior, and conduct of the people of Paradise.

A "Salam" that has made that environment heavenly, where every kind of annoyance and harm has been removed.

A "Salam" that is a sign of a secure and safe environment, an environment full of sincerity, intimacy, purity, piety, peace, and tranquility.

In other verses of the Quran, this same reality is mentioned with different expressions. In Surah Az-Zumar (Verse 73), we read: وَ قالَ لَهُمْ خَزَنَتُها سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ طِبْتُمْ فَادْخُلُوها خالِدِينَ‌: "Its keepers will say to them: Peace be upon you; you have become pure, so enter it to abide eternally!" In Surah Qaf (Verse 34), we read: ادْخُلُوها بِسَلامٍ ذلِكَ يَوْمُ الْخُلُودِ: "Enter it in peace and safety; that is the Day of Eternity."

Not only do the angels send greetings upon them and they to one another, but Allah Himself also sends peace upon them, as stated in Surah Ya-Sin (Verse 58): سَلامٌ قَوْلًا مِنْ رَبٍّ رَحِيمٍ‌: "Peace!—a word from a Merciful Lord [to you, the people of Paradise]."

Is there any environment more serene and beautiful than this environment filled with peace and safety?

Following this blessing, it points to another blessing, saying: "And they will have their provision therein, morning and evening" (وَ لَهُمْ رِزْقُهُمْ فِيها بُكْرَةً وَ عَشِيًّا).

This sentence raises two questions:

First: Is there morning and evening in Paradise?

The answer to this question in Islamic narrations is as follows: Although there is constant light and brightness in Paradise, the people of Paradise distinguish day and night by the increasing and decreasing of the light and its fluctuations.

The second question: It is clearly understood from the verses of the Quran that the people of Paradise have whatever they desire of bounties and provisions available to them always and at every hour; what then is this provision that comes to them only morning and evening?

The answer to this question can be found in a subtle Hadith narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله where he says:

"And exquisite gifts from Allah the Almighty are given to them at the times they used to perform prayer in this world." [^1]

From this Hadith, it is understood that these distinguished gifts—the nature of which cannot be described even by guess or estimation—are highly valuable blessings that are presented to them morning and evening in addition to the usual heavenly blessings.

Does the expression of the above verse and the Hadith mentioned not indicate that the life of the people of Paradise is not monotonous, but rather every day, every morning, and every evening, a new bounty and a fresh grace encompasses them?

And does this not imply that man's evolutionary journey will continue there? Although he performs no new deeds there, he will continue his evolutionary path using the vehicle he prepared from his beliefs and actions in this world.

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 16, p. 103.

After the brief description of Paradise and its material and spiritual blessings, it introduces the people of Paradise in one short sentence, saying: "That is the Paradise which We give as an inheritance to those of Our servants who were God-fearing" (تِلْكَ الْجَنَّةُ الَّتِي نُورِثُ مِنْ عِبادِنا مَنْ كانَ تَقِيًّا).

In this way, the key to the door of Paradise, with all those aforementioned blessings, is nothing but "Piety" (Taqwa).

Although the expression "Our servants" (عبادنا) gives a brief indication of faith and piety, this is not a place to suffice with a brief indication; rather, this reality must be stated explicitly: that Paradise is only the place for the pious.

Again, here we encounter the word "inheritance" (Urth), which is usually applied to property transferred from one person to another after death, whereas Paradise did not belong to anyone else and there is apparently no transfer involved.

The answer to this question can be given in two ways:

1- "Inheritance" (Urth) in linguistic terms also means "to grant ownership" and is not limited to the transfer of property from the deceased to the survivors.

2- In a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"There is no one except that he has a residence in Paradise and a residence in Hell. As for the disbeliever, he inherits the believer's residence in Hell, and the believer inherits the disbeliever's residence in Paradise!" [^1]

It is also necessary to mention that "inheritance" in the sense mentioned in the Hadith is not based on genealogical ties, but rather on the ties of school of thought and the practical application of piety.

From the occasion of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) that some commentators have narrated regarding the above verse, the same meaning is understood: one of the polytheists named "As ibn Wa'il" did not pay the wages of his worker (who was apparently a Muslim man) and mockingly said: "If what Muhammad says is true, we are more deserving of the blessings of Paradise than anyone else; I will pay this worker's wages in full there!" The above verse was revealed and said: "This Paradise is specifically for the pious servants."

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 31 (We have also discussed this in Vol. 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, p. 177).

Ayahs

وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ ۖ لَهُۥ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا وَمَا بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا (٦٤)
[O Gabriel, tell the Prophet,] ‘We do not descend except by the command of your Lord. To Him belongs whatever is before us and whatever is behind us and whatever is in between that, and your Lord does not forget (64)
رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فَٱعْبُدْهُ وَٱصْطَبِرْ لِعِبَـٰدَتِهِۦ ۚ هَلْ تَعْلَمُ لَهُۥ سَمِيًّا (٦٥)
—the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them. So worship Him and be steadfast in His worship. Do you know anyone who could be His namesake?’ (65)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators have stated regarding the occasion of revelation for the above verses that for several days, revelation ceased, and Gabriel, the messenger of Divine revelation, did not come to the Prophet. When this period passed and Gabriel descended upon the Prophet, he asked him: "Why did you delay? I was very longing for you." Gabriel replied: "I am more longing for you! But I am a commissioned servant; when I am commanded, I come, and when I have no instruction, I refrain." [^1]

Commentary

We are Servants with Soul and Heart in Hand

Although there is a specific occasion of revelation for these verses as mentioned above, this does not prevent them from having a logical connection and link with the preceding verses. This is because they serve as an emphasis that everything Gabriel brought in the previous verses is entirely and without deficiency from Allah, and nothing is from himself. The first verse, speaking from the tongue of the messenger of revelation, says:

"And we [angels] descend not except by the command of your Lord" (وَ ما نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ).

Everything is from Him, and we are servants with soul and heart in hand. "To Him belongs what is before us and what is behind us and what is between that" (لَهُ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِينا وَ ما خَلْفَنا وَ ما بَيْنَ ذلِكَ).

In short, the future, the past, the present, here, there, everywhere, this world, the hereafter, and the Barzakh (isthmus) all belong to the Pure Essence of the Lord.

And know this as well, that "your Lord has never been forgetful" (وَ ما كانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا).

Some commentators have mentioned various interpretations for the phrase "To Him belongs what is before us and what is behind us and what is between that," which occasionally reach eleven interpretations, but what we mentioned above seems most appropriate of all.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 6, p. 4168; and Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan under the verse in question (with slight variations).

Then He adds: All of these are by the command of your Lord, "The Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them" (رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما).

Now that this is the case and all lines lead to Him, "So worship Him alone" (فَاعْبُدْهُ).

A worship coupled with monotheism and sincerity. And since in this path—the path of servitude, obedience, and sincere worship of Allah—there are many difficulties and hardships, He adds: "And be constant and patient in His worship" (وَ اصْطَبِرْ لِعِبادَتِهِ).

And in the final sentence, He says: "Do you know of any likeness or namesake for Him?" (هَلْ تَعْلَمُ لَهُ سَمِيًّا).

This sentence is, in fact, a proof for what appeared in the previous sentence; meaning, is there any partner or peer for His Pure Essence that you would reach out to him and worship him?! Although the word "Samiy" (سَمِيًّا) means "namesake," it is clear that the intention here is not merely the name, but the content of the name. That is, will you find anyone other than Allah who is the Creator, the Provider, the Giver of Life, the Bringer of Death, All-Knowing, and All-Powerful over everything?

Ayahs

وَيَقُولُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَءِذَا مَا مِتُّ لَسَوْفَ أُخْرَجُ حَيًّا (٦٦)
Man says, ‘What? Shall I be brought forth alive [from the grave], when I have been dead?’ (66)
أَوَلَا يَذْكُرُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ يَكُ شَيْـًٔا (٦٧)
Does not man remember that We created him before when he was nothing? (67)
فَوَرَبِّكَ لَنَحْشُرَنَّهُمْ وَٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ ثُمَّ لَنُحْضِرَنَّهُمْ حَوْلَ جَهَنَّمَ جِثِيًّا (٦٨)
By your Lord, We will surely gather them with the devils; then We will surely bring them up around hell [scrambling] on their knees. (68)
ثُمَّ لَنَنزِعَنَّ مِن كُلِّ شِيعَةٍ أَيُّهُمْ أَشَدُّ عَلَى ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عِتِيًّا (٦٩)
Then from every group We shall draw whichever of them was more defiant towards the All-beneficent. (69)
ثُمَّ لَنَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِهَا صِلِيًّا (٧٠)
Then surely We will know best those who deserve most to enter it. (70)

Occasion of Revelation

The initial verses—according to a group of commentators—were revealed concerning "Ubayy ibn Khalaf" or "Walid ibn al-Mughirah," who held a piece of decayed bone in their hands, crushing it into powder and scattering it into the wind so that every particle of it would be dispersed. They would say: "Look at Muhammad, who imagines that Allah will bring us back to life after we have died and our bones have decayed like this bone; such a thing is absolutely impossible!"

These verses were revealed and provided a crushing response to them—a response that is beneficial and instructive for all human beings across all centuries and ages.

Commentary

A Description of the Dwellers of Hell

In the preceding verses, significant discussions were raised regarding the Resurrection, Paradise, and Hell; the verses under discussion continue on the same subject.

In the first verse, it recounts the speech of the deniers of the Resurrection: "And man says: 'When I am dead, shall I truly be brought forth alive?'" (وَ يَقُولُ الْإِنْسانُ أَ إِذا ما مِتُّ لَسَوْفَ أُخْرَجُ حَيًّا).

Of course, this inquiry is a rhetorical question of denial, meaning "such a thing is impossible." However, the use of the term "man" (especially with the definite article al- denoting the genus), even though it would have been appropriate to use "disbeliever" instead, is perhaps because this question is more or less latent in the nature of every human being initially. Upon hearing the issue of "life after death," a question mark is immediately drawn in his mind.

Immediately, with the same tone and expression, He answers him: "Does man not remember that We created him before, while he was nothing at all?" (أَ وَ لا يَذْكُرُ الْإِنْسانُ أَنَّا خَلَقْناهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَ لَمْ يَكُ شَيْئاً).

The use of "man" here may also point to the fact that man, with his God-given talent and intelligence, should not remain silent before such a question; he should answer it himself by recalling his first creation, otherwise, he has not utilized his true "humanity."

These verses, like many verses related to the Resurrection, emphasize the physical Resurrection; otherwise, if only the soul were to remain and the return of the body to life were not an issue, neither that question would have been relevant nor this answer.

In any case, this logic which the Quran employs here to prove the Resurrection has appeared in other places in the Quran as well, including at the end of Surah Ya-Sin, where it is presented with the same term "man": "Does man not consider that We created him from a sperm-drop? Then at once he is a clear adversary. And he presents for Us an example and forgets his [own] creation. He says, 'Who will give life to bones while they are disintegrated?' Say, 'He will give them life who produced them the first time; and He is, of all creation, Knowing'" (Surah Ya-Sin, 77-79). [1] Some commentators have raised a question here: if this argument is correct—that whoever has performed a task has the power to perform its like—then

[1]: Regarding this argument, we have discussed it in Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 144, under the title "The Shortest Argument for the Resurrection" (and also in Volume 12 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 152 onwards).

why is it that we perform certain tasks but sometimes cannot repeat the exact same thing? For example, we might compose a very sublime piece of poetry or write with very beautiful calligraphy, but later, no matter how hard we try, we cannot do the same.

The answer we have chosen for this question is that while it is true we perform our actions out of will and choice, sometimes a series of involuntary factors affect certain characteristics of our actions. An imperceptible tremor of our hand may affect the precise shape of letters. Furthermore, our power and talent are not always the same; sometimes factors arise that mobilize all our inner strength, and we can create what is called a masterpiece, but at other times the motivating factors are weak and our full strength is not mobilized. For this reason, the second attempt is not performed as well as the first.

However, for Allah, whose power is infinite, such issues do not exist for Him; whatever He performs, He can repeat the exact same thing without any deficiency.

In the next verse, with a very decisive tone, He threatens the deniers of the Resurrection and the faithless sinners, saying: "So by your Lord, We will surely gather them and the devils [who used to whisper to them or were their deities]" (فَوَ رَبِّكَ لَنَحْشُرَنَّهُمْ وَ الشَّياطِينَ). "Then We will surely bring them around Hell on their knees" (ثُمَّ لَنُحْضِرَنَّهُمْ حَوْلَ جَهَنَّمَ جِثِيًّا).

This verse indicates that the court for the faithless and sinful individuals is near Hell!

The expression "jithiyya" (considering that "jithiyy" is the plural of "jathi," meaning one who is sitting on his knees) perhaps refers to their weakness, helplessness, humiliation, and abasement; as if they do not have the strength to stand on their feet. Of course, this word has other meanings as well, including that some have interpreted "jithiyy" to mean "group by group," and some as "heaped and piled upon one another" like soil and stones, but the first interpretation is more appropriate and well-known.

And since priorities are observed in that court of justice, He says in the next verse: We will first go after the most rebellious and defiant individuals: "Then We will surely extract from every sect those of them who were worst against the Most Merciful in insolence" (ثُمَّ لَنَنْزِعَنَّ مِنْ كُلِّ شِيعَةٍ أَيُّهُمْ أَشَدُّ عَلَى الرَّحْمنِ عِتِيًّا). [1] Those very shameless ones who even consigned the favors of the "Most Merciful" God to oblivion and rose in audacity, rebellion, and defiance against their Benefactor; yes, these are the most deserving of all for the fire of Hell!

Again, emphasizing this meaning, He says: "Then, surely it is We who are most knowing of those most worthy of being burned therein" (ثُمَّ لَنَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِالَّذِينَ هُمْ أَوْلى‌ بِها صِلِيًّا). We select them precisely, and no mistake will occur in this selection.

"Siliyy" is a verbal noun that has come to mean both the lighting of a fire and that which is burned by fire.

[1]: "Shi'ah" in its original linguistic sense means a group that cooperates with one another to perform a task, and the choice of this expression in the above verse may indicate that these faithless misguided ones cooperated with each other in the path of rebellion and insolence, and We shall first settle the account of that group which was the most rebellious of all.

Ayahs

وَإِن مِّنكُمْ إِلَّا وَارِدُهَا ۚ كَانَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ حَتْمًا مَّقْضِيًّا (٧١)
There is none of you but will come to it: a [matter that is a] decided certainty with your Lord. (71)
ثُمَّ نُنَجِّى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّنَذَرُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ فِيهَا جِثِيًّا (٧٢)
Then We will deliver those who are Godwary, and leave the wrongdoers in it, fallen on their knees. (72)

Commentary

Everyone Enters Hell?!

The above verses continue the discussion regarding the characteristics of the Resurrection and the rewards and punishments.

First, it points to a matter that might be astonishing for most people to hear, saying: "Every one of you, without exception, shall enter it [Hell]" (وَ إِنْ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا وارِدُها).

"This is an inevitability and a decreed command from your Lord" (كانَ عَلى‌ رَبِّكَ حَتْماً مَقْضِيًّا).

"Then We shall save those who practiced piety, and We shall leave the wrongdoers and oppressors in it, fallen on their knees [out of weakness and humiliation]" (ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَ نَذَرُ الظَّالِمِينَ فِيها جِثِيًّا).

In the commentary of these two verses, there is an extensive discussion among commentators based on what is meant by "entry" (ورود) in the phrase "إِنْ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا وارِدُها".

Some commentators believe that "entry" here means approaching and overlooking. That is, all people—the good and the bad—without exception, will come to the edge of Hell for the reckoning of accounts or to witness the final fate of the evildoers. Then, Allah will grant deliverance to the God-fearing and leave the oppressors within it.

For this interpretation, they argue using verse 23 of Surah Al-Qasas: وَ لَمَّا وَرَدَ ماءَ مَدْيَنَ... "And when he arrived at the water of Madyan..." where "entry" (ورود) is used in the same sense of arriving at a place.

The second interpretation, which the majority of commentators have chosen, is that "entry" here means "entering into." Accordingly, all human beings without exception, both good and bad, will enter Hell. However, the Fire will be cool and safe for the righteous, just as the fire of Nimrod was for Ibrahim عليه السلام (يا نارُ كُونِي بَرْداً وَ سَلاماً عَلى‌ إِبْراهِيمَ‌). This is because the Fire has no affinity with them; it is as if it distances itself and flees from them, and wherever they stand, it is extinguished. But the denizens of Hell, who possess an affinity with the Fire, will immediately ignite like flammable material reaching a flame.

Regardless of the philosophy behind this—which we will explain later, God willing—the apparent meaning of the verse undoubtedly aligns with the second interpretation. This is because the primary meaning of wurud is "entering," and any other meaning requires supporting evidence. Furthermore, the phrase "ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا" (Then We shall save those who practiced piety), much like the phrase "نَذَرُ الظَّالِمِينَ فِيها" (We shall leave the oppressors in it), serves as evidence for this meaning.

Additionally, numerous narrations have been transmitted regarding the commentary of this verse that fully reinforce this meaning:

Among them, it is narrated from Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari that someone asked him about this verse. Jabir pointed to both his ears with his fingers and said:

"I heard something with these two ears of mine from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله such that if I lie, may both become deaf! He said:

الورود الدخول، لا يبقى بر و لا فاجر لا يدخلها فيكون على المؤمنين بردا و سلاما كما كانت على ابراهيم حتى ان للنار- او قال لجهنم- ضجيجا من بردها، ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَ نَذَرُ الظَّالِمِينَ فِيها جِثِيًّا:

'Entry here means entering into. There is no righteous or wicked person except that he shall enter Hell. The Fire will be cool and safe for the believers, just as it was for Ibrahim, to the extent that the Fire—or Hell (Jabir was uncertain)—will cry out from its coldness. Then Allah will deliver the God-fearing and leave the oppressors therein in a state of humiliation.'" [^1]

In another hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

تقول النار للمؤمن يوم القيامه جز، يا مؤمن! فقد اطفا نورك لهبى:

"The Fire will say to the believer on the Day of Resurrection: 'Pass through quickly, O believer! For your light has extinguished my flame!'" [^2]

This meaning is also derived from several other narrations.

The profound description found in narrations regarding the Bridge of Sirat—that it is stretched over Hell, is thinner than a hair and sharper than a sword—is another witness and proof for this interpretation. [^3]

As for those who say that verse 101 of Surah Al-Anbiya: أُولئِكَ عَنْها مُبْعَدُونَ‌ ("They [the believers] shall be kept far away from it") is evidence for the first interpretation, it does not seem correct. This is because that verse refers to the permanent abode and eternal dwelling of the believers. In the following verse, we even read: لا يَسْمَعُونَ حَسِيسَها: "The believers will not even hear the sound of its flames."

If "entry" in the verse under discussion meant merely approaching, it would be consistent neither with the word "kept far away" (mub'adun) nor with the phrase "they will not hear its sound."

Answer to a Question [The Philosophy of Everyone Entering Hell]

The only question that remains here is: what is the philosophy behind this action in terms of the Wisdom of the Lord? Furthermore, will the believers not suffer pain or torment from this?

The answer to this question, which has been addressed from both aspects in Islamic narrations, becomes clear with a little reflection:

In reality, witnessing Hell and its torments will be a prelude for the believers to derive the maximum pleasure from the God-given blessings of Paradise. For "one only knows the value of well-being when one is afflicted by calamity" (and things are known by their opposites). Here, the believers are not afflicted by calamity; rather, they only witness the scene of calamity. As we read in the aforementioned narrations, the Fire becomes cool and safe for them, and their light overshadows the flame of the Fire.

Moreover, they pass through the Fire so swiftly and decisively that it cannot have the slightest effect on them. As narrated in a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

يرد الناس النار ثم يصدرون باعمالهم فاولهم كلمع البرق، ثم كمر الريح، ثم كحضر الفرس، ثم كالراكب، ثم كشد الرجل، ثم كمشيه:

"All people will enter the Fire (Hell), then they will emerge from it according to their deeds. The first of them will be like a flash of lightning, then like the passing of a gale, then like a galloping horse, then like a normal rider, then like a man running fast, and then like one walking normally." [^1]

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 353.

Furthermore, the denizens of Hell will suffer even greater punishment by witnessing the scene of the people of Paradise passing through with such speed while they remain behind. Thus, the answer to both questions becomes clear.

[^1] and [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, pp. 352-353.

[^3]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 572, under the verse إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصادِ (Al-Fajr: 14).

Ayahs

وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَىُّ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ خَيْرٌ مَّقَامًا وَأَحْسَنُ نَدِيًّا (٧٣)
When Our manifest signs are recited to them, the faithless say to the faithful, ‘Which of the two groups is superior in station and better with respect to company?’ (73)
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْنٍ هُمْ أَحْسَنُ أَثَـٰثًا وَرِءْيًا (٧٤)
How many a generation We have destroyed before them, who were superior in furnishings and appearance! (74)
قُلْ مَن كَانَ فِى ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةِ فَلْيَمْدُدْ لَهُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ مَدًّا ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا رَأَوْا۟ مَا يُوعَدُونَ إِمَّا ٱلْعَذَابَ وَإِمَّا ٱلسَّاعَةَ فَسَيَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا وَأَضْعَفُ جُندًا (٧٥)
Say, ‘Whoever abides in error, the All-beneficent shall prolong his respite until they sight what they have been promised: either punishment, or the Hour.’ Then they will know whose position is worse, and whose host is weaker. (75)
وَيَزِيدُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱهْتَدَوْا۟ هُدًى ۗ وَٱلْبَـٰقِيَـٰتُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتُ خَيْرٌ عِندَ رَبِّكَ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ مَّرَدًّا (٧٦)
Allah enhances in guidance those who are [rightly] guided, and lasting righteous deeds are better with your Lord in reward, and better at the return [to Allah]. (76)

Commentary

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the faithless oppressors, the verses under discussion describe a portion of their logic and their ultimate fate.

We know that the first group to believe in the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله were the pure-hearted downtrodden (mustad'afin) who were empty-handed of worldly wealth and possessions—the same deprived and oppressed group for whose salvation from the clutches of tyrants and oppressors the divine religions were revealed; high-minded men and women of faith such as Bilal, Salman, Ammar, Khabbab, Sumayyah, and their like.

Since the criterion of value in the Ignorant (Jahili) society of that time—as in any other ignorant society—was gold, ornaments, money, status, and outward appearance, the oppressive wealthy, such as "Nadr ibn al-Harith" and his ilk, would boast over the group of poor believers, saying: "The sign of our personality is with us, and the sign of your lack of personality is your very poverty and deprivation!"

They claimed: "This itself is proof of our being on the right path and your being on the wrong one!"

As the Quran states in the first verse under discussion: "And when Our clear verses are recited to them, those who disbelieve say to those who believe, 'Which of the two parties is better in station and more beautiful in assembly?'" (وَ إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا بَيِّناتٍ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَيُّ الْفَرِيقَيْنِ خَيْرٌ مَقاماً وَ أَحْسَنُ نَدِيًّا).

Specifically, we read in Islamic narrations that this aristocratic and affluent group would wear their most beautiful clothes and adorn themselves, walking in front of the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله while casting looks of contempt and mockery at them. Indeed, this is the way and custom of this class in every age and time.

[The Meaning of "Nadi" and "Nadiyyan"]

"Nadi" is originally derived from "nada" meaning moisture, and subsequently came to mean eloquent and speaking individuals.

This is because one of the conditions for the ability to speak is having sufficient saliva; thereafter, "nida" came to mean sitting together and speaking, and even a gathering where a group assembles for intimacy or consultation is called "nadi." The "Dar al-Nadwa," which was a place in Mecca where leaders gathered and consulted, is derived from this same meaning.

Furthermore, generosity and bestowal are sometimes expressed as "nada" [^1], and the above verse may refer to all of these. That is: "Our gathering of intimacy is more beautiful than yours, our money, wealth, glitter, and clothing are more attractive, and our eloquent and fluent speeches and poems are better and more far-reaching!"

But the Quran, with a fully reasoned yet decisive and crushing statement, answers them thus: It seems they have forgotten the past history of mankind: "And how many a generation have We destroyed before them who were better in possessions and outward appearance?" (وَ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا قَبْلَهُمْ مِنْ قَرْنٍ هُمْ أَحْسَنُ أَثاثاً وَ رِءْياً). [^2] Did their money, wealth, glittering assemblies, luxurious clothes, and beautiful faces manage to prevent the divine punishment? If these were proof of their character and status before God, why did they meet such a grim fate? The glitter of the world is so unstable that with a slight breeze, not only is its record disturbed, but sometimes its entire scroll is rolled up.

[^1]: Mufradat al-Raghib, root "n-d-y".

[^2]: "Athath" means the goods and ornaments of the world, and "ri'yan" means form and appearance. "Qarn"—as we have stated previously (Volume 5, page 156)—usually means a long period of time, but since it is derived from the root "iqtiran" meaning proximity, it is also applied to a people or population gathered in one time.

Then He gives them another warning, saying: "Tell them, O faithless oppressors, do not imagine that this wealth and property of yours is a source of mercy; rather, it may well be the cause of divine punishment: 'Whoever is in error and insists on continuing this path, the Most Merciful will extend for him a [long] delay, and this affluent life will continue as such'" (قُلْ مَنْ كانَ فِي الضَّلالَةِ فَلْيَمْدُدْ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ مَدًّا). "Until they see what they were promised: either the punishment [in this world] or the Hour [of Resurrection]" (حَتَّى إِذا رَأَوْا ما يُوعَدُونَ إِمَّا الْعَذابَ وَ إِمَّا السَّاعَةَ).

"That is the day they will know who is worse in position and who is weaker in forces" (فَسَيَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ شَرٌّ مَكاناً وَ أَضْعَفُ جُنْداً).

In reality, such deviant individuals who are no longer capable of guidance (note that the Quran says "مَنْ كانَ فِي الضَّلالَةِ" which refers to persistence in misguidance), in order to face the most painful divine punishment, Allah sometimes immerses them in blessings. This both becomes a source of their pride and negligence, and makes the divine punishment of stripping away those blessings more painful. This is the very thing mentioned in some verses of the Quran as the punishment of "istidraj" (gradual leading to ruin). [^1] The phrase "فَلْيَمْدُدْ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ مَدًّا", although in the form of a command, is in the sense of a report, and its meaning is that Allah gives them respite and continues their blessings.

Some commentators have also interpreted it in the literal sense of a command, which here would imply a curse—

[^1]: Refer to Volume 7 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 32 (under verses 182 and 183 of Surah Al-A'raf).

—or in the sense of the necessity of such behavior from God, but the first interpretation seems closer [to the truth].

The word "adhab" (punishment) in the above verse, by the context of being placed opposite to "al-Sa'ah" (the Hour), refers to divine punishments in the world—punishments such as the Flood of Noah, the earthquake and heavenly stones that descended upon the people of Lot, or punishments that fall upon them at the hands of the believers and warriors of the front of truth, as we read (in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 14): "قاتِلُوهُمْ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ اللَّهُ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ‌": "Fight them; Allah will punish them by your hands."

And "al-Sa'ah" here either means the end of the world or the divine punishment in the Resurrection (the second meaning seems more appropriate).

This is the end and fate of the oppressors and those deceived by the glitter and pleasures of the world. "But those who have stepped onto the path of guidance, Allah increases them in guidance" (وَ يَزِيدُ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ اهْتَدَوْا هُدىً‌). It is obvious that guidance has degrees; when the first degrees are traversed by a person, Allah takes his hand and leads him to higher degrees. Like a fruitful tree that undergoes a new stage of growth and perfection every day, these guided ones also step into higher stages every day in the light of their faith and righteous deeds.

At the end of the verse, He answers those who relied on their fleeting ornaments in the world and made them a means of boasting over others, saying: "The righteous deeds that remain (al-baqiyat al-salihat) are better in the sight of your Lord for reward and better for return" (وَ الْباقِياتُ الصَّالِحاتُ خَيْرٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ ثَواباً وَ خَيْرٌ مَرَدًّا). [^1]

[^1]: "Maradd" (on the weight of namad—with a doubled 'd') is either an infinitive meaning "return" or a noun of place meaning "place of return," which here refers to Paradise; however, the first possibility is more consistent with the meaning of the verse.

Ayahs

أَفَرَءَيْتَ ٱلَّذِى كَفَرَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَقَالَ لَأُوتَيَنَّ مَالًا وَوَلَدًا (٧٧)
Have you not regarded him who defies Our signs, and says, ‘I will surely be given wealth and children’? (77)
أَطَّلَعَ ٱلْغَيْبَ أَمِ ٱتَّخَذَ عِندَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَهْدًا (٧٨)
Has he come to know the Unseen, or taken a promise from the All-beneficent? (78)
كَلَّا ۚ سَنَكْتُبُ مَا يَقُولُ وَنَمُدُّ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ مَدًّا (٧٩)
No indeed! We will write down what he says, and We will prolong his punishment endlessly. (79)
وَنَرِثُهُۥ مَا يَقُولُ وَيَأْتِينَا فَرْدًا (٨٠)
We shall take over from him what he talks about, and he will come to Us alone. (80)
وَٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ءَالِهَةً لِّيَكُونُوا۟ لَهُمْ عِزًّا (٨١)
They have taken gods besides Allah that they may be a [source of] might to them. (81)
كَلَّا ۚ سَيَكْفُرُونَ بِعِبَادَتِهِمْ وَيَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضِدًّا (٨٢)
No Indeed! Soon they will disown their worship, and they will be their opponents. (82)

Commentary

A Superstitious and Deviant Thought

Some people believe that faith, purity, and piety are incompatible with their interests! They think these qualities cause the world to turn its back on them, whereas by exiting the sphere of faith and piety, the world will favor them, and their wealth and riches will increase.

Whether this way of thinking stems from naivety and following superstitions, or serves as a cover to escape the burden of Divine responsibilities and commitments, it is a dangerous mindset.

We have sometimes seen these superstitious individuals use the wealth of some faithless people and the poverty and deprivation of a group of believers as a pretext to prove this superstition. This is while we know that neither the wealth obtained through oppression, disbelief, and the abandonment of the principles of piety is a source of pride, nor are faith and God-wariness ever a barrier to legitimate and permissible activities.

In any case, during the era of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله—just like our own time—there were ignorant individuals who held such a notion or at least pretended to.

In the verses under discussion—fitting with the previous discussion regarding the fate of the disbelievers and oppressors—the Quran speaks of this mindset and its consequence.

In the first verse, He says: "Have you seen him who disbelieved in Our signs and said, 'I will surely be given wealth and children'?" (أَ فَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي كَفَرَ بِآياتِنا وَ قالَ لَأُوتَيَنَّ مالًا وَ وَلَداً). [1]

[1]: Some commentators have narrated a "reason for revelation" (shan nuzul) for the above verse: that one of the believers named "Khabbab" had a debt owed to him by one of the polytheists named "As ibn Wa'il." The debtor said mockingly, "In the next world, when I acquire wealth and children, I will pay back your debt." However, in our view, this reason for revelation does not align well with the verse under discussion, especially since children are mentioned, and we know that children are not a matter of concern in the Hereafter. Furthermore, in the subsequent verses, it explicitly states: "We shall inherit from him what he says," which clearly indicates that the reference is to worldly wealth, not wealth in the Hereafter. In any case, a group of commentators, based on this reason for revelation, considered the verse to refer to the Hereafter, but the truth is what has been stated.

Then the Quran responds to them as such: "Has he looked into the Unseen, or has he taken a covenant with the All-Beneficent?" (أَطَّلَعَ الْغَيْبَ أَمِ اتَّخَذَ عِنْدَ الرَّحْمنِ عَهْداً).

Only someone who is aware of the Unseen could make such a prediction and claim a connection between disbelief and the acquisition of wealth and children—since we see no such connection—or he must have taken a covenant from Allah, which is also a meaningless claim.

Then, with a decisive tone, He adds: "By no means! (Never shall disbelief and lack of faith be the cause of increase in anyone's wealth and children.) We will soon record what he says" (كَلَّا سَنَكْتُبُ ما يَقُولُ‌). Yes, these baseless words, which might cause the deviation of some naive people, will all be recorded in their record of deeds.

"And We will extend for him the punishment" (successive punishments, one after another) (وَ نَمُدُّ لَهُ مِنَ الْعَذابِ مَدًّا).

This phrase might refer to the continuous and eternal punishment of the Hereafter, or it might refer to the punishments that afflict them in this world due to disbelief and lack of faith. It is also worth considering the possibility that this wealth and these children, which are sources of pride and misguidance, are themselves a continuous punishment for them!

"And We shall inherit from him what he says (of wealth and children), and he will come to Us all alone on the Day of Resurrection" (وَ نَرِثُهُ ما يَقُولُ وَ يَأْتِينا فَرْداً).

Yes, eventually he leaves all these material facilities behind and goes, appearing before the court of Divine justice empty-handed, while his record of deeds is black with sins and void of good deeds. It is there that he sees the result of his baseless claims in the world.

The next verse refers to another motivation of these individuals in worshipping idols, saying: "And they have taken gods besides Allah that they might be a source of honor for them" (وَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ آلِهَةً لِيَكُونُوا لَهُمْ عِزًّا). So that they might intercede for them before Allah and help them in times of trouble. But what a wrong notion and what a vain imagination!

It is never as they imagined; not only will the idols not be a source of honor for them, but they will be the source of humiliation and punishment. For this reason, "Soon—that is, on the Day of Resurrection—the deities will deny the worship of the worshippers and disown them; rather, they will be against them" (كَلَّا سَيَكْفُرُونَ بِعِبادَتِهِمْ وَ يَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضِدًّا). This sentence refers to the same point we read in Surah Fatir, verse 14:

وَ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ ما يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْ قِطْمِيرٍ إِنْ تَدْعُوهُمْ لا يَسْمَعُوا دُعاءَكُمْ‌ ...وَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكْفُرُونَ بِشِرْكِكُمْ‌: "And those whom you invoke besides Him do not possess even the skin of a date-stone. If you invoke them, they do not hear your call... and on the Day of Resurrection, they will deny your association of them with Allah."

And also in Surah Al-Ahqaf, verse 6, we read: وَ إِذا حُشِرَ النَّاسُ كانُوا لَهُمْ أَعْداءً:

"And when the people are gathered, they [the deities] will be enemies to them."

Some great commentators have also suggested the possibility that the above verse means that the idol worshippers, on the Day of Resurrection when the veils are removed and all realities become manifest and they see themselves disgraced, will deny worshipping the idols and speak against them. As we read in Surah Al-An'am, verse 23, the idolaters will say on the Day of Resurrection: وَ اللَّهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ‌: "By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists!"

However, the first interpretation is more consistent with the literal meaning of the verse, because the worshippers wanted the deities to be their source of honor, but eventually, they become their opponents.

Of course, the situation of deities such as angels, or satans and jinn who possess intellect and understanding, is clear. But as for inanimate deities, on that day, they may speak by Allah's command and declare their disavowal of the worshippers.

The same interpretation can be derived from a hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, for the Imam says regarding the interpretation of the above verse:

"Those whom they took as gods besides Allah will be opponents on the Day of Resurrection, and they will disown them and their worship until the Day of Resurrection."

Interestingly, at the end of the hadith, we read a short and profound sentence about the reality of worship:

"Worship is not (only) prostration and bowing; rather, the reality of worship is the obedience to men. Whoever obeys a creature in disobedience to the Creator has indeed worshipped him" (and his fate is the same as that of the polytheists and idolaters). [1]

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 357.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ تَؤُزُّهُمْ أَزًّا (٨٣)
Have you not regarded that We unleash the devils upon the faithless to urge them vigorously? (83)
فَلَا تَعْجَلْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ إِنَّمَا نَعُدُّ لَهُمْ عَدًّا (٨٤)
So do not make haste against them; indeed We are counting for them, a counting [down]. (84)
يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ وَفْدًا (٨٥)
The day We shall gather the Godwary toward the All-beneficent, on mounts, (85)
وَنَسُوقُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ وِرْدًا (٨٦)
and drive the guilty as a thirsty herd towards hell, (86)
لَّا يَمْلِكُونَ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةَ إِلَّا مَنِ ٱتَّخَذَ عِندَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَهْدًا (٨٧)
no one will have the power to intercede [with Allah], except for him who has taken a covenant with the All-beneficent. (87)

Commentary

Who is Eligible for Intercession?

In light of the discussion in the preceding verses regarding the polytheists, the verses under discussion actually point to some of the causes of their deviation and subsequently their grim fate and end. They also establish the fact that these deities were not only not a source of honor for them, but rather became a cause of their misery and humiliation.

First, It says: "Have you not seen that We have sent the devils upon the disbelievers, inciting them [to sin] with [constant] incitement?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ أَنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا الشَّياطِينَ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ تَؤُزُّهُمْ أَزًّا).

The word "Azz" (أزّ), as Raghib states in Al-Mufradat, originally means the boiling of a pot and the churning of its contents during intense ebullition. Here, it is a metaphor for the devils gaining such mastery over them that they move them in any direction and in any form they desire, turning them upside down!

It is obvious—as we have stated many times—that the mastery of devils over humans is not a forced or unconscious mastery; rather, it is the human who allows the devils to enter his heart and soul, places the collar of their servitude around his neck, and accepts their obedience. As the Quran says in Surah An-Nahl, verse 100: "His authority is only over those who take him as a patron and those who through him associate partners [with Allah]" (إِنَّما سُلْطانُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُ وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ بِهِ مُشْرِكُونَ‌).

Then, addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, It says: "So be not in haste against them; We are only counting out for them a [limited] number" (فَلا تَعْجَلْ عَلَيْهِمْ إِنَّما نَعُدُّ لَهُمْ عَدًّا). We shall record and preserve everything for that day when the court of Divine Justice is convened.

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that "counting" refers to the days of their lives, or rather their very breaths; meaning their period of remaining is short and subject to numbering and counting, for something being "numbered" is usually a metaphor for it being little and short.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of "إِنَّما نَعُدُّ لَهُمْ عَدًّا", we read:

He asked one of his companions: "In your opinion, what is the Lord counting in this verse?" He replied: "The number of days." The Imam said: "Fathers and mothers also keep count of the days of their children's lives,

wa lakinahu 'adad al-anfas:

but rather it is the counting of the number of breaths!" [1] This expression of the Imam may refer to the first interpretation, the second, or both.

In any case, reflecting on the content of this verse shakes a person, for it proves that everything of ours, even our breaths, is accounted for and numbered, and one day we must answer for all of them.

Then, It describes the final destination of the "pious" and the "criminals" in short and eloquent phrases: "We have stored all these deeds for the day when We shall gather the righteous to the Most Merciful as a delegation [of honor]" (يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ الْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى الرَّحْمنِ وَفْداً).

The word "Wafd" (on the pattern of Wa'd) originally refers to a delegation or group that goes to great personages to solve their problems and is received with respect and honor. Therefore, it implicitly carries the concept of respect, and perhaps this is why we read in some narrations that the pious will be mounted on swift mounts and proceed to Paradise with great honor.

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: Ali عليه السلام asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله about the interpretation of this verse (يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ الْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى الرَّحْمنِ وَفْداً). He said:

"O Ali! A Wafd can only be

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 357.

those who are mounted. They are individuals who adopted piety and God-wariness; Allah loved them, chose them for Himself, was pleased with their deeds, and named them the pious..." [1]

It is noteworthy that in the above verse we read: He takes the pious toward "the Most Merciful" (al-Rahman), while in the next verse the talk is of "driving" the criminals to Hell. Would it not have been more appropriate to say "Paradise" here instead of "the Most Merciful"? However, this expression actually points to an important point: that the pious find something higher than Paradise there; they draw near to the station of proximity to God and His special manifestations, and they perceive His pleasure, which is the highest Paradise (the expressions we read in the aforementioned hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله also allude to this meaning).

Then It says: In contrast, "We will drive the criminals to Hell like a thirsty herd" (وَ نَسُوقُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ إِلى‌ جَهَنَّمَ وِرْداً). Just as thirsty camels are driven toward a watering hole, except that here there is no water, but fire.

It should be noted that the word "Wird" refers to a group of humans or animals entering a watering hole, and since such a group is certainly thirsty, commentators have taken this expression here to mean "the thirsty."

What a distance there is between those who are taken with honor and respect toward the Most Merciful God, with angels rushing to welcome them and sending peace and blessings upon them,

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 359.

and a group that is driven like thirsty animals toward the fire of Hell, while they are downcast, ashamed, disgraced, and worthless?

And if they imagine that they can achieve anything there through intercession, they must know that "they will not possess [the power of] intercession" (لا يَمْلِكُونَ الشَّفاعَةَ). Neither will anyone intercede for them, nor—by greater reason—are they capable of interceding for anyone.

"Except those who have taken a covenant with the Most Merciful" (إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّخَذَ عِنْدَ الرَّحْمنِ عَهْداً).

Only this group will be included in the intercession of the intercessors, or their status is even higher than this, and they have the ability to intercede for sinners who are worthy of intercession.

What is the meaning of "Covenant" (Ahd)?

Commentators have discussed extensively what is meant by "covenant" in the above verse, which says only those who have a covenant with God possess intercession.

Some have said: "Covenant" is faith in the Lord, acknowledgment of His Oneness, and confirmation of God's Prophets.

Others have said: "Covenant" here means testifying to the Oneness of the Truth, disavowing those who believe in any refuge or power against God, and also not having hope in other than "Allah."

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, in response to one of his friends who asked about the interpretation of the above verse, said:

"Whoever believes in the Wilayah (guardianship) of the Commander of the Faithful and the Imams after him, that is the covenant with Allah." [1]

In another narration from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Whoever brings joy to a believer has brought joy to me, and whoever brings joy to me has taken a covenant with Allah." [2]

In another hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that guarding the covenant is the same as guarding the five daily prayers. 3

From an examination of the above narrations found in various Islamic sources, as well as the words of the great commentators of Islam, we conclude that "covenant with God"—as understood from its literal meaning—has a broad sense that encompasses every kind of relationship with the Lord, His recognition (Ma'rifah), and obedience to Him, as well as the connection and bond with the school of the Guardians of Truth (Awliya), and every kind of righteous deed, although each narration points to a part of it or a clear instance of it.

Therefore, in another hadith narrated from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله regarding how to make a will, almost all matters of belief are gathered, where he said:

A Muslim should make such a will on the threshold of death, saying: "O Lord! You are the Creator of the heavens and the earth, Knower of the unseen and the manifest, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate. I pledge to You in this world and bear witness that there is no deity but You, You are One, You have no partner, Muhammad is Your servant and Your Messenger, Paradise is true, Hell is true, the Resurrection and the Account are true, Divine Decree and the Scale for weighing deeds are true,

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 362. [2]: Al-Durr al-Manthur (as cited in Al-Mizan under the verse discussed).

religion is as You have described, Islam is as You have legislated, the Word is as You have spoken, the Quran is as You have revealed, You are the Manifest Truth. O Lord! Reward Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله on our behalf with the best reward and send salutations and peace upon him and his family.

O Lord! You are my treasure in difficulties and my helper in hardships; You are the Bestower of my blessings; You are my God and the God of my fathers. Do not leave me to myself for the blink of an eye, for if You leave me, I will draw near to evil and far from good. O my God! Be my companion in the grave and establish for me a covenant that I may find open on the Day of Resurrection."

Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "After confessing to these truths, he bequeaths what he deems necessary, and the confirmation of this will is in Surah Maryam in the verse: 'They will not possess [the power of] intercession except those who have taken a covenant with the Most Merciful.' This is the covenant and the will..." [1]

Obviously, the intention is not that one should merely recite or write the above matters in Arabic or Persian like incantations, but rather believe in them with all his heart—a faith whose effects become manifest in his entire life program.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَلَدًا (٨٨)
They say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken a son!’ (88)
لَّقَدْ جِئْتُمْ شَيْـًٔا إِدًّا (٨٩)
You have certainly advanced something hideous! (89)
تَكَادُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِنْهُ وَتَنشَقُّ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَتَخِرُّ ٱلْجِبَالُ هَدًّا (٩٠)
The heavens are about to be rent apart at it, the earth to split open, and the mountains to collapse into bits, (90)
أَن دَعَوْا۟ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ وَلَدًا (٩١)
that they should ascribe a son to the All-beneficent! (91)
وَمَا يَنۢبَغِى لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ أَن يَتَّخِذَ وَلَدًا (٩٢)
It does not behoove the All-beneficent to take a son. (92)
إِن كُلُّ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّآ ءَاتِى ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَبْدًا (٩٣)
There is none in the heavens and the earth but he comes to the All-beneficent as a servant. (93)
لَّقَدْ أَحْصَىٰهُمْ وَعَدَّهُمْ عَدًّا (٩٤)
Certainly He has counted them [all] and numbered them precisely, (94)
وَكُلُّهُمْ ءَاتِيهِ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فَرْدًا (٩٥)
and each of them will come to Him alone on the Day of Resurrection. (95)

Commentary

Allah and Having a Child?!!

Since the preceding verses discussed polytheism and the ultimate fate of the polytheists, at the conclusion of this discussion, it points to one of the branches of polytheism—namely, the belief in the existence of a child for God—and recounts the ugliness of this claim with the most decisive expression:

"And they say: 'The Most Merciful has taken a son'!" (وَ قالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمنُ وَلَداً).

It was not only the Christians who believed that Prophet "Messiah" was the literal son of God; the Jews held a similar belief regarding "Ezra" and the idolaters regarding the "angels," whom they imagined to be the daughters of Allah. [^1]

Then, with a crushing tone, He says: "Indeed you have brought forth a monstrous thing" (لَقَدْ جِئْتُمْ شَيْئاً إِدًّا). "Idd" (rhyming with zidd) originally refers to an ugly and discordant sound heard from a camel's throat due to the intense vibration of sound waves; subsequently, it has been applied to extremely heinous and terrifying deeds.

Since such an improper attribution contradicts the principle of Monotheism (Tawhid)—because Allah

[^1]: Regarding "Ezra," it is mentioned in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 30, and regarding the "angels," it is discussed under verse 19 of Surah Az-Zukhruf.

has neither peer nor likeness, nor any need for a child, nor the accidents of body and physicality—it is as if the entire realm of existence, which is founded upon Monotheism, sinks into horror and agitation at this improper attribution.

Therefore, in the next verse, He adds: "The heavens are almost ruptured therefrom and the earth split open and the mountains fall in devastation" (تَكادُ السَّماواتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِنْهُ وَ تَنْشَقُّ الْأَرْضُ وَ تَخِرُّ الْجِبالُ هَدًّا).

Again, to emphasize and express the gravity of the matter, He says: "That they should attribute to the Most Merciful a son" (أَنْ دَعَوْا لِلرَّحْمنِ وَلَداً).

In reality, these people have not recognized Allah at all; otherwise, they would know that "it is not appropriate for the Most Merciful that He should take a son" (وَ ما يَنْبَغِي لِلرَّحْمنِ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ وَلَداً). A human desires a child for one of several reasons:

Either because his life will come to an end, and he needs procreation for the survival of his lineage.

Or he seeks a helper and supporter because his strength is limited.

Or he fears loneliness and seeks a companion for his solitude.

Or in old age and infirmity, he wants a young helper and assistant.

However, none of these concepts have any meaning regarding Allah; His power is not limited, His life does not end, weakness and frailty find no way into His being, nor does He feel loneliness or need.

Furthermore, having a child is a sign of being a physical body and having a spouse, and all these meanings are far removed from His Pure Essence.

For this reason, in the next verse, He says: "There is no one in the heavens and earth but that he comes to the Most Merciful as a servant" (إِنْ كُلُّ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا آتِي الرَّحْمنِ عَبْداً). And while all servants have their lives and hearts in His hand, He has no need for their obedience or compliance; rather, it is they who are in need.

"He has enumerated them and counted them a [full] counting" (لَقَدْ أَحْصاهُمْ وَ عَدَّهُمْ عَدًّا).

That is, never imagine how He could keep account of them despite such a vast number of servants; His knowledge is so vast and expansive that He not only has their number but is aware of all their characteristics. They can neither escape the domain of His sovereignty, nor is anything of their deeds hidden from Him.

"And all of them are coming to Him on the Day of Resurrection alone" (وَ كُلُّهُمْ آتِيهِ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فَرْداً). Therefore, the Messiah, Ezra, the angels, and all human beings are included in this general decree of His. Despite this, how unseemly it is to attribute a child to Him, and how much we bring His Pure Essence down from the heights of grandeur and deny His attributes of Majesty and Beauty if we attribute sonship to Him. [^1]

[^1]: Regarding the negation of a child for Allah, we have also discussed this in Volume 1 under verse 116 of Al-Baqarah, and in Volume 8 under verse 68.

Key Points

1- They Still Consider Him the Son of God!

What we read in the above verses negates a child from Allah with the most decisive words. These are verses from fourteen centuries ago, yet today, in the world of science and knowledge, there are still many who consider the Messiah عليه السلام the son of God—not a metaphorical son, but a literal one! And if in some writings intended for proselytization and specifically tailored for Islamic environments, they present this sonship as ceremonial or metaphorical, it is by no means consistent with the primary texts of their creedal books.

This matter is not limited to the sonship of the Messiah عليه السلام; regarding the issue of the Trinity—which certainly means three gods and is part of their definitive beliefs—because Muslims are horrified by hearing such polytheistic words, they change their tone in Islamic environments and justify it through types of simile and metaphor.

(For further explanation, refer to the Bible Dictionary regarding "Messiah" and the "Three Persons/Hypostases").

2- How Do the Heavens Rupture?

What we read in the above verses: "The heavens are almost ruptured from this improper attribution, and the earths split open and the mountains fall," is either an indication that, based on the expressions of the Holy Quran, the entire realm of existence possesses a kind of life, perception, and consciousness. Verses such as verse 74 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "And indeed, there are some of them which fall down for fear of Allah" (وَإِنَّ مِنْها لَما يَهْبِطُ مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ‌), and verse 21 of Surah Al-Hashr: "If We had sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled and coming apart from fear of Allah" (لَوْ أَنْزَلْنا هذَا الْقُرْآنَ عَلى‌ جَبَلٍ لَرَأَيْتَهُ خاشِعاً مُتَصَدِّعاً مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ‌), serve as evidence for this.

This improper attribution to the Holy Presence of the Lord plunges the entire world into deep horror.

Alternatively, it is a metaphor for its extraordinary ugliness. Similar metaphors are not uncommon in the Arabic and Persian languages, where we say, "You did something such that it was as if the heavens and earth were crushed upon my head."

God willing, we will discuss this further under appropriate verses.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَيَجْعَلُ لَهُمُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وُدًّا (٩٦)
Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds—the All-beneficent will endear them [to His creation]. (96)
فَإِنَّمَا يَسَّرْنَـٰهُ بِلِسَانِكَ لِتُبَشِّرَ بِهِ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ وَتُنذِرَ بِهِۦ قَوْمًا لُّدًّا (٩٧)
Indeed We have made it simple in your language so that you may give good news thereby to the Godwary and warn with it a disputatious lot. (97)
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْنٍ هَلْ تُحِسُّ مِنْهُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ أَوْ تَسْمَعُ لَهُمْ رِكْزًۢا (٩٨)
How many a generation We have destroyed before them! Can you descry any one of them, or hear from them so much as a murmur? (98)

Commentary

Faith, the Fountainhead of Popularity!

In these three verses, which conclude Surah Maryam, the discourse once again turns to the faithful believers and the faithless oppressors and tyrants. It speaks of the Quran, its glad tidings, and its warnings; in reality, it is an essence of the preceding discussions, enriched with fresh insights.

First, He says: "Indeed, those who have attained faith and performed righteous deeds, the Most Compassionate will appoint for them affection [in the hearts of people]" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَيَجْعَلُ لَهُمُ الرَّحْمنُ وُدًّا).

Some commentators have considered this verse to be specifically about the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, while others consider it to encompass all believers.

Some have suggested that it means Allah casts their love into the hearts of their enemies, and this love becomes a tether around their necks that draws them toward faith.

Others have interpreted it as the mutual love between believers, which results in power, strength, and unity of word.

Some have viewed it as an allusion to the friendship of believers toward one another in the Hereafter, stating that they will become so fond of each other that meeting one another will bring them the highest joy and delight.

However, if we contemplate the vast concepts of the verse with a broad perspective, we will see that all these interpretations are gathered within the meaning of the verse without contradicting one another.

The core point is that "faith and righteous deeds" possess an extraordinary attraction and pull. Belief in the Oneness of God and the call of the Prophets—the reflection of which manifests in a person's soul, thought, speech, and action in the form of high human ethics, piety, purity, integrity, trustworthiness, courage, sacrifice, and forgiveness—acts like a massive magnetic force, drawing and captivating others.

Even impure and tainted individuals find pleasure in the pure and detest the impure like themselves. For this reason, when they wish to choose a spouse or a partner, for instance, they insist that the other party be pure, noble, trustworthy, and honest.

This is natural and is, in fact, the first reward that Allah grants to the believers and the righteous, the scope of which extends from this world to the next abode.

We have often seen with our own eyes that when such pure individuals close their eyes to this world, eyes weep for them, even if they apparently held no social rank or position. All people feel their absence, and everyone considers themselves a partner in mourning them.

As for the fact that some have attributed this to the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام—as mentioned in many narrations—there is no doubt that its highest degree and loftiest stage are exclusive to that Imam of the Pious (and we will discuss these narrations in detail in the Key Points). However, this does not prevent all believers and righteous people from tasting this love and popularity in public opinion at other levels and receiving a share of this divine affection. Nor does it prevent enemies from feeling a sense of love and respect for them in their own hearts.

Interestingly, we read in a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Indeed, when Allah loves a servant, He calls Gabriel and says: 'O Gabriel, I love so-and-so, so love him.' Gabriel then loves him and announces to the inhabitants of heaven: 'Allah loves so-and-so, so love him.' Consequently, the inhabitants of heaven love him, and then acceptance for him is placed on the earth. And when Allah hates a servant, He calls Gabriel and says: 'O Gabriel, I hate so-and-so, so hate him.' Gabriel then hates him and announces to the inhabitants of heaven: 'Allah hates so-and-so, so hate him.' Consequently, the inhabitants of heaven hate him, and then hatred for him is placed on the earth!" [^1]

This profound Hadith shows that faith and righteous deeds have a reflection as vast as the universe, and the radius of popularity resulting from them encompasses the entire expanse of creation. The Pure Essence of Allah loves such people; they are beloved by all the inhabitants of heaven, and this love radiates into the hearts of humans on earth.

Truly, what pleasure is greater than for a person to feel they are beloved by all the pure and good beings of the universe? And how painful it is for a person to feel that the earth and sky, the angels, and the faithful humans all detest and loathe them.

Then, referring to the "Quran," which is the source of nurturing faith and righteous deeds, He says: "We have made it [the Quran] easy in your tongue only so that you may give glad tidings with it to the pious and warn with it a contentious people."

[^1]: This Hadith is found in many famous sources of Hadith as well as many books of Tafsir, but we have chosen the text quoted in Tafsir "Fi Zilal," Vol. 5, p. 454, from "Ahmad," "Muslim," and "Bukhari."

(فَإِنَّما يَسَّرْناهُ بِلِسانِكَ لِتُبَشِّرَ بِهِ الْمُتَّقِينَ وَ تُنْذِرَ بِهِ قَوْماً لُدًّا).

"Ludd" (with a damma on the 'l' and shadda on the 'd') is the plural of "Aladd" (on the pattern of 'Adad'), meaning an enemy who possesses intense hostility. It is used for those who are fanatical, stubborn, and illogical in their enmity.

The final verse, as a consolation to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers (especially considering that this Surah was revealed in Mecca when Muslims were under severe pressure), and also as a threat and warning to all spiteful and stubborn enemies, says:

"And how many a generation have We destroyed before them! Do you perceive any one of them or hear from them even a whisper?" (وَ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا قَبْلَهُمْ مِنْ قَرْنٍ هَلْ تُحِسُّ مِنْهُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ أَوْ تَسْمَعُ لَهُمْ رِكْزاً).

"Rikz" means a faint sound, and things hidden underground are called "Rikaz." This means these oppressive nations and stubborn enemies of truth and reality were so crushed that not even a faint sound is heard from them.

Key Points

1- The Love of Ali (عليه السلام) in the Hearts of Believers

In many books of Hadith and Tafsir by Sunni scholars (in addition to Shia), numerous narrations regarding the occasion of revelation of the verse (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَيَجْعَلُ لَهُمُ الرَّحْمنُ وُدًّا) have been narrated from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, showing that this verse was first revealed regarding Ali عليه السلام. Among these scholars, one can name "Allamah Zamakhshari" in Al-Kashshaf, "Sibt ibn al-Jawzi" in Tadhkirah, "Ganji Shafi'i," "Qurtubi" in his famous Tafsir, "Muhibb al-Din Tabari" in Dhakha'ir al-Uqba, "Nishapuri" in his well-known Tafsir, "Ibn Sabbagh Maliki" in Al-Fusul al-Muhimmah, "Suyuti" in Al-Durr al-Manthur, "Haythami" in Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah, and "Alusi" in Ruh al-Ma'ani. Examples include:

1- "Tha'labi" narrates in his Tafsir from "Bara' ibn Azib": The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said to Ali عليه السلام: "Say: 'O Allah, grant me a covenant with You, and grant me affection in the hearts of the believers.' Then Allah the Almighty revealed: 'Indeed, those who have attained faith and performed righteous deeds, the Most Compassionate will appoint for them affection.'" [^1] The exact same wording or with slight variations appears in many other books.

2- In many Islamic books, this meaning is narrated from Ibn Abbas, who says: "It was revealed concerning Ali ibn Abi Talib: 'Indeed, those who have attained faith and performed righteous deeds, the Most Compassionate will appoint for them affection.' He said: 'Love in the hearts of the believers.'" [^2]

3- In the book Al-Sawa'iq, it is narrated from "Muhammad ibn al-Hanafiyyah" regarding the interpretation of this verse: "There remains no believer except that in his heart there is love for Ali and his household." [^3]

4- Perhaps for this reason, it is narrated in an authentic and reliable report from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام himself: "If I were to strike the nose of a believer with this sword of mine so that he might hate me, he would never hate me; and if I were to pour the whole world with its riches upon a hypocrite so that he might love me, he would never love me. That is because it was a decree passed upon the tongue of the Unlettered Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله when he said: 'O Ali! No believer will hate you, and no hypocrite will love you!'" [^4]

[^1] and [^2]: According to the report in Ihqaq al-Haq, Vol. 3, pp. 83-86. [^3]: Same source. [^4]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 16, p. 130; Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 6, p. 533; and also Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 45.

5- In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, at the end of his prayer, would pray for the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in a loud voice so that the people could hear: "O Allah! Grant Ali affection in the chests of the believers, and awe and greatness in the chests of the hypocrites." Then the above verse and the verse following it were revealed. [^1]

In any case, as we stated in the commentary of the above verses, the revelation of this verse regarding Ali عليه السلام is as a supreme and perfect example, and it does not prevent the generalization of its concept to all believers according to their ranks.

2- Interpretation of the Phrase "We have made it easy in your tongue"

"Yassarnahu" is from the root "Taysir," meaning to facilitate. Allah says in this sentence: "We have made the Quran easy in your tongue so that you may give glad tidings to the pious and warn the stubborn enemies." This facilitation may be from various aspects:

1- In the sense that the Quran is in eloquent and fluent Arabic, whose melody is delightful to the ears and its recitation is easy on the tongues.

2- In the sense that Allah granted His Prophet such mastery over the verses of the Quran that he could use them easily everywhere and for solving every problem, and he would constantly recite them to the believers.

3- In terms of content, which, while being deep and rich, is easy and simple to understand. Fundamentally, the fact that all those great and prominent truths are cast into the mold of these limited words with such ease of understanding is itself a sign of what we read in the above verse—that it occurred through divine assistance.

In Surah Al-Qamar, this sentence is repeated in several verses: "And We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?"

O Lord! Illumine our hearts with the light of faith and our entire being with the light of righteous deeds. Make us lovers of the believers and the righteous, especially the Imam of the Pious, the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, and cast our love into the hearts of all believers.

O God! Gather our great Islamic community—which, despite having such numbers and vast material and spiritual resources, is caught in the clutches of enemies and has become weak and powerless due to the scattering and division of ranks—around the torch of faith and righteous deeds.

O Allah! Just as You destroyed and obliterated the tyrants and oppressors of the past such that not the slightest sound is heard from them, destroy the arrogant superpowers of our time as well. Cut off their evil from the oppressed and bring the uprising of the believers against these arrogant ones to final victory.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Maryam Friday, February 12, 1982 (23 Bahman 1360) 17 Rabi' al-Thani 1402
